So now I wanna give you guys a sort of a cool bonus video release strategy that has worked on my videos, and that is when you schedule your videos to release at a time that is not on the hour or it's not on the half hour mark. The reason for this is because a lot of different YouTube channels, a lot of the channels out there are going to release their videos exactly on the hour or on the half hours. So it could be like 7:00 PM or 7:30 PM. So you're essentially competing with those channels if they post at the exact same time as you. To select a payment option on OnlyFans&nbsp; for withdrawing your earnings, you need to go&nbsp;&nbsp; through the platform's payout settings. Here’s a&nbsp; step-by-step guide on how to choose and set up a&nbsp;&nbsp; payment method on OnlyFans: Once logged in, click&nbsp; on your profile icon located at the top right of&nbsp;&nbsp; the page. In the dropdown menu, click on “Bank”&nbsp; to manage your payment options. Select Your Payout&nbsp;&nbsp; Method In the Payout Methods section, you will&nbsp; see various payment options that OnlyFans supports How to withdraw money from OnlyFans? By&nbsp; the default you have Manual payouts and&nbsp;&nbsp; you can withdraw any minimum amount of money&nbsp; allowed by your payment provider. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can also use OnlyFans Weekly Payouts&nbsp; or withdraw your earnings each month. At this point take a look at&nbsp; OnlyFans current and pending balance The Current Balance is the amount of money that&nbsp; is fully cleared and available for withdrawal.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is your usable earnings, meaning you can&nbsp; request to transfer this amount to your bank&nbsp;&nbsp; account or payment method at any time.The&nbsp; Pending Balance consists of your earnings&nbsp;&nbsp; that are still within the holding period&nbsp; and are not yet available for withdrawal.&nbsp;&nbsp; This balance is essentially money that&nbsp; you've earned but that hasn't been fully&nbsp;&nbsp; processed by OnlyFans. OnlyFans holds&nbsp; earnings in the Pending Balance for a&nbsp;&nbsp; certain period (typically 7 days) to account for&nbsp; potential chargebacks, refunds, or fraud checks. Let's withdraw our hard-earned money&nbsp; from OnlyFans together. Press Request&nbsp;&nbsp; Withdrawal and input the amount you want to&nbsp; withdraw, in our case we select max amount. Once the payment be proceeded, you will receive&nbsp; the amount on your selected payment method. Chargebacks on OnlyFans can be a significant&nbsp; concern for creators, impacting their earnings&nbsp;&nbsp; and overall financial stability on the platform.&nbsp; Here’s a detailed overview of how chargebacks&nbsp;&nbsp; work on OnlyFans, their implications, and&nbsp; how creators can manage them effectively. What is a Chargeback? A chargeback occurs&nbsp;&nbsp; when a customer disputes a transaction&nbsp; with their bank or credit card issuer,&nbsp;&nbsp; leading to the reversal of the transaction.&nbsp; This can happen for various reasons, such as: Unauthorized Transactions: The customer&nbsp; claims they did not authorize the purchase.&nbsp; Fraudulent Activity: The customer suspects their&nbsp;&nbsp; account has been compromised. Dissatisfaction with Content:&nbsp;&nbsp; The customer feels the content did not meet&nbsp; expectations or was not delivered as promised.&nbsp; Technical Issues: Problems with the platform&nbsp; or access to content can lead to disputes. OnlyFans offers a variety of payout&nbsp; methods for creators around the world,&nbsp;&nbsp; allowing for flexibility in how they&nbsp; receive their earnings. Each method&nbsp;&nbsp; has its own advantages and fees,&nbsp; making it essential for creators&nbsp;&nbsp; to choose the best option based on&nbsp; their location and banking needs. Number three, let's move on to creating your Only account and verifying your account, which are very different things. Creating an account is one thing, verifying it as a content creator is another . So, to verify your Only account as a creator, they'll ask for your ID or scan your face or Face ID. They will perform facial verification to authenticate that you are the person opening the account and to confirm that it is your identity document. You will also scan your ID from the front and back. Depending on the country you are in, there are different document options to verify your account. The options include a national identity card or ID, a foreign resident document, a passport, or a driver's license . With any of these options you can verify your Only account as a creator. Some people say it's much easier with a passport, and others say it's not. I recommend you do it with whatever you have on hand. You simply have to follow the corresponding steps, take a photo where your photo is visible within the document and where the data is clearly visible. I recommend that you also attach the photo that Only asks for, holding your document, as extra content, because at the end of the account verification form, Only has an option where you can add information that can help validate or authenticate the information already provided. In this section you can also attach public utility bills, whether for electricity or telephone line, where the same address appears as you already entered in the form. Once you have submitted the request to verify your account as a content creator, you should expect to wait 24 to 72 hours for a response. Point number four. Once your account is verified, you will determine the subscription price. Yef lets you set prices from $5 to $50 per monthly subscription, because there are other ways to make money within this platform, but we talk about that in other videos, so I invite you to watch them. However, I want to make it clear that the global average used by content creators for monthly subscriptions is $1 to $1. This does not mean that you can place more or less. You set the value. I'd like to take this opportunity to tell you that in the description we've included our link with valuable information, whether you're a model or an agency owner. There you will find tools and private groups that can accelerate your growth. Point five. The money you earn within Only is not money that you keep 100% of. And I'll explain why. Because the Only platform takes a 20% commission on all the money you generate. It may sound very demotivating, but if you think about it, 20% is not really relevant. This is something you should keep in mind if you are going to start in this industry. Point number six. If you are going to create content in OF, in collaboration or with another person, whoever that may be, there are two ways to do it. One, that person creates an account and verifies it as a content creator. Or two, you can send a form that only has this person's information and declare that they accept the permissions to appear in this video. In the description I'll leave you a link to that form, which is a template you can use if that person doesn't want to create an OF account. Point number seven, what ways are there to earn money in OF? We have a video specifically talking about how to make money on this platform, so I'll leave it on a card for you, but I'll mention it briefly. You can earn money through monthly subscriptions, tips, or gratuities on your photos that you upload to your feed or wall. You can also earn money for personalized content, through messages, or through live streams or video calls that you make within this platform. Another source of income, but not directly with Only, is if you are already an established creator, to charge new creators for recommending them within your platform. It's also worth mentioning something else that's very important: you can also earn money by unlocking posts. Point number eight, configure a welcome message. When a user subscribes to your Only account, they expect a warm welcome, and Only allows you to schedule a message so that every time someone subscribes, they receive that message. You configure that welcome message. You can give them a photo or a video or something else you want to give them for subscribing to your account. You can greet them or ask them how they found your account or how long they have been following you on your social networks. The important thing here in the welcome message is to motivate the user to start talking to you because messaging is one of the ways to make more money within Only. Of course you make money through subscriptions, but you can make five or ten times more through messaging than you earn through subscriptions. Point number nine. Now we're going to talk about how to promote your OF. For this, I recommend that you create previews or small snippets of the content you create for your Only account. Yes, with censorship. There are some people I really don't understand why they do it that way, showing absolutely everything. At Tipo, we neither do it nor recommend it because 'only' means exclusivity. If someone wants to see explicit content, they go to one of those X-rated sites. But the premise, that special touch that Only has compared to those sites, is the homemade and exclusive content. It is the fact that the content provides a closeness with the creator. So, I don't understand why there are creators who directly share previews or teasers showing absolutely everything. The post might go viral , but in terms of subscribers, if someone already sees all your content in that post, why would they pay? Why would they subscribe to your OnlyFans if they already watched it for free? So, you're going to release spicy teasers that don't show everything. Don't give away your content and be patient because when you're good at your content, you'll start to attract attention and go viral, especially when there's no explicit content out there, because entering your OnlyFan will become more exclusive. So, you're going to upload these censored previews? You can post them on Twitter or X or on Reddit. Formats like Guess the Country by Emoji are a blast in shots. As the timer runs on the screen, the viewer is forced to wait for the answer and then write their score in the comments. Algorithms love two things the most. Watch time and comments. Both are available here for free. Open Canva, pick a quiz template, enter questions, set a timer, and reveal the answers. Just repeat. These aren't pie in the sky dreams. These are proven realistic income streams you can start with today. Affiliate marketing, your I don't want to make my own product starter kit. Share your personalized link and when someone buys, kaching, you get paid. No shipping, no customer complaints, no caring emails. A real world example for this would be sign up for programs like Amazon Associates or ClickBank. Post product reviews on Tik Tok, Instagram, or a blog. Add your affiliate link and boom, you're in business. Tip: Don't promote $2 pens. Go for the big fish. Software, tech, high ticket courses. Bigger the product, bigger the payday. Coming in hot at number two, YouTube automation. Make videos without ever showing your face. Yes, you can be a YouTuber without dancing, vlogging, or even speaking. You create or outsource scripts, get a voice over, add some B-roll or animations, and post. That's YouTube automation. Now, what makes this powerful? Channels in high-paying niches, finance, crypto, tech, can make thousands each month just from ads and affiliate links. Numerous faceless finance channel make $10,000 a month and the creator doesn't even have a webcam. Genius. Sliding in at number three, digital products. Build once, sell forever. We're talking ebooks, courses, templates, notion dashboards, Excel planners, you name it. Digital products are the gift that keeps on giving you money. Example, create a debt-free in 90 days ebook or a budgeting notion template. Upload it to Gumroad, Etsy, or your own website. Promote on social media or Pinterest, and every sale puts money directly in your pocket, even while you sleep. Next up, number four, drop shipping and print on demand. The low-risk e-commerce hustle. No need to stock products in your garage or beg friends to buy your merch. With drop shipping, customers order, your supplier ships, and you just collect the difference. Popular move. Start a Shopify store. Use Sprocket or Printify to handle orders. Create niche t-shirts, mugs, or home decor. One viral dog mom energy hoodie could cover your rent. Rolling in at number five, social media management. Get paid to do what you're already doing for free. You scroll Instagram. You make memes. You know what's trending. Why not get paid for it? Here's how. Offer content creation and account management services to small businesses. A lot of them have no clue how to grow online. You could charge $1,000 a month per client. Just five clients equals $5,000 a month, and you never have to leave your house. At number six, we've got influencer sponsorships. You don't need a million followers. Even micro influencers are cashing in. If you've got 5,000 plus engaged followers in a specific niche, brands will pay you to post. Fun fact, a creator with just 10,000 followers in the cooking niche landed $750 for one sponsored Tik Tok showing how to use a fancy blender. She didn't even blend anything. She just vibed with it. And finally, number seven, AI powered side hustles. The future is now. Thanks to tools like Chat GPT, Midjourney, and Canva AI, you can create content, write copy, generate art, and even build business plans. Killer idea. Offer AI generated blog posts or logos on Fiverr or Upwork. You can charge $50 to $100 per deliverable. Knock out five in a day and you're looking at a solid $300 plus with AI doing most of the heavy lifting. All right, so now you've got the blueprint, but let's not just file this video under cool stuff I watched once at 2 a.m. Let's get you actually started. Here's how you go from passive watcher to active money maker. Step one, pick one strategy. Just one. Seriously, don't be like me back in 2020, juggling 11 different side hustles at once, sleeping 4 hours a night and crying into a $6 kombucha because none of them were working. The biggest mistake beginners make is trying to do everything at once. dropshipping, YouTube, affiliate marketing, crypto, dog walking, underwater basket weaving. You name it, I tried it. Start with one income stream, master it, then scale or expand later. The internet isn't going anywhere. There's no need to panic hustle your way into burnout. Step two, research your niche like a nerd. Stalk. Yes, stalk the people who are already crushing it in your chosen lane. What kind of content do they post? What offers are they promoting? What are people asking in their comments? This is your digital detective phase. Don't copy, but analyze. Patterns reveal gold. Use tools like YouTube search, Google trends, Tik Tok hashtags, Reddit forums, whatever helps you understand your future audience and customers. Step three, start. Just start. This is the part where most people freeze. They want the logo to be perfect, the website to be gorgeous, the Instagram feed to look like it was curated by a Vogue editor. Nah, you're not launching a $100 million brand in one day. You're building momentum. Perfection is the enemy of progress. Record that first video, write that first blog post, post that cringy first Tik Tok. You'll get better as you go, but only if you go. Step four. Maybe that means hiring a Fiverr freelancer to edit your videos or using tools like Canva, Zapier, or Buffer to schedule and streamline your content. Once the system works, your job becomes CEO. Not doing everything, but managing the machine. That's where the real freedom starts to kick in. Now, final reality check. You're not here to be perfect. You're here to be profitable. Forget the filters. Forget the haters. Forget the fear. The difference between you and the people already making money online, they started. So, take a deep breath and take action. Before you dive head first into the digital money pool, let's talk about the floaties that'll keep you from drowning. Because while making money online is very possible, it's also very easy to mess up if you're not careful. Here are the biggest mistakes that tank more online dreams than bad Wi-Fi connections. Mistake number one, shiny object syndrome. You know that feeling when you're super hyped about affiliate marketing until you see someone on Tik Tok making $10,000 a month from vending machines and suddenly you're researching coin operated entrepreneurship at 3:00 a.m. That's shiny object syndrome. Every new hustle looks better than the one you're working on. But here's the truth. All these business models can work. The problem isn't the hustle. It's the hustle hopper. If you switch strategies every time a new trend pops up, you'll never give yourself the time to master any of them. Pick one, stick with it for at least 90 days. Focus, execute, adjust. That's the path. Mistake number two, underpricing your work. Listen, if you're charging $5 for a full logo design, you're not running a business. You're volunteering for capitalism. You're not doing favors here. You're providing value. Pricing isn't just about what you think your work is worth. It's about communicating quality to your client. If your pricing looks like a discount rack at a sketchy gas station, people will treat your work accordingly. Instead, know your value. If you're offering a service that helps someone make money, save time, or look better online, charge like it. They're not just paying for your time. They're paying for your skill, your experience, and your energy. Mistake number three, ignoring tools. You can try to do everything manually, but that's like deciding to walk across the country instead of hopping in a car because you don't want to spend on gas. Tools like Canva for design, Notion for organization, TubeBuddy for YouTube optimization, or Jasper AI for writing. They're investments, not expenses. A good tool can save you hours every week, level up your output, and make you look way more professional. and a lot of them have free or cheap starter plans. There's no badge of honor for suffering through it. Work smart. Mistake number four, forgetting SEO. If a tree falls in a forest and no one's around to hear it, does it make a sound? And if you post the best blog, video, or product in the world, but no one finds it because you didn't use keywords, does it make you money? Nope. SEO or search engine optimization is the secret sauce that gets your content in front of eyeballs. Whether it's YouTube, Tik Tok, Pinterest, or Google, understanding how to title, tag, and structure your content can be the difference between three views and 30,000. Learn basic SEO practices. Use keywords your target audience is searching for. Think like a customer, not a creator. The algorithm isn't your enemy. It's your marketing department and it works for free if you feed it right. All these mistakes boil down to one thing, rushing the process. Building online income is like building a house. Lay your foundation. Don't cut corners. And definitely don't switch blueprints halfway through. You've got the tools. Now, just avoid the traps. Now, big conferences like this, it can be a lot of money to sign up for. So what I personally actually prefer is to do vendor fairs where the tables are much cheaper. Oftentimes, you're the only author at the event, so if there's any book nerds at the event, they gravitate towards your table and buy your book. Whereas at a big expo like Book Con, you know, you're one of hundreds of authors. So readers have a lot more decisions to make when it comes to shopping for a book. Whereas if you're at a vendor fair and you're the only author at the event and a reader just already likes your book, they're probably gonna be more likely to buy it. You can also host virtual book signings. So this is just doing it all virtually where you have a stock of books at your house and people can buy it online. Now, that's the tricky part. - Over 60? Do This Reverse Tabletop Exercise Every Day for Better Strength: Kind: captions Language: en Have you ever noticed that after the age of 60, certain movements that once felt easy start to feel a little harder? Standing up from the floor, reaching behind you, or maintaining good posture for long periods can slowly become more challenging. Many adults believe this is simply a normal part of getting older. But in many cases, the real reason is something much simpler. Certain muscles gradually stop working together the way they once did. One of the biggest mistakes people over 60 make about exercise is believing it has to be exhausting to be effective. For years, many of us were taught that if a workout doesn't leave you out of breath or covered in sweat, it doesn't really count. The idea of exercise became connected with intense gym routines, heavy weights, or pushing the body to its limits. But for many adults over 60, that belief quietly becomes the reason they stop moving altogether. When exercise feels too difficult, too complicated, or even intimidating, it's easy to think maybe it's just not meant for you anymore. But the truth is something very different. After 60, the body actually needs movement more than ever. As we age, we naturally begin losing muscle strength, joint mobility, and posture stability. Without regular movement, muscles that once supported everyday activities slowly become less active. The encouraging news is that protecting your strength and independence later in life does not require extreme workouts. In fact, research in aging and functional fitness shows that simple body weight movements can significantly improve mobility, posture, and overall strength in older [snorts] adults. And today we're going to explore one simple exercise that many people overlook but can be surprisingly powerful. It's called the reverse tabletop exercise. It requires no equipment, very little space, and when practiced regularly, it can activate important muscles that often become weaker with age. Before we continue, we'd love to know a little about you. If you're watching this video and you're over 60, write a comment below with your name, your age, and where you're watching from. Our community is filled with inspiring adults from around the world who are choosing to stay strong and active after 60. Now, let's talk about why this simple exercise can make such a difference. One of the most overlooked changes that happens with aging is not just muscle loss, but muscle inactivity. Certain muscles gradually stop participating in everyday movement. The glutes become less active after years of sitting. The muscles between the shoulder blades weaken. The back side of the body, often called the posterior chain, slowly becomes less engaged. When this happens, the body begins compensating. The lower back starts working harder than it should. The shoulders roll forward. Standing up, lifting objects, or maintaining good posture may begin to feel more difficult. The reverse tabletop exercise helps gently reawaken these muscles. Instead of targeting just one muscle group, it activates several important areas at the same time, including the glutes, shoulders, upper back, and core. These muscles work together to lift and stabilize the body, which is exactly how they function during everyday movement. When the body practices movements like this, it begins to remember how to move as a connected system again. Another important benefit of this exercise is improved shoulder mobility. Many adults notice that after 60, their shoulders feel tighter than they used to. Reaching overhead or moving the arms behind the body can start to feel stiff. Daily habits like sitting at a desk, driving, or using a phone often cause the shoulders to move forward or the chest becomes tighter. Over time, this pattern can limit mobility and affect posture. When performing the reverse tabletop exercise, the shoulders move gently behind the body while the chest opens. This helps activate the muscles in the upper back that support the shoulder blades and improve overall shoulder stability. Another major benefit is improved posture. Many people assume poor posture is simply part of aging, but it is often caused by muscle imbalance. The muscles that support upright posture gradually weaken while other muscles become overworked. The reverse tabletop helps strengthen the muscles that support a tall and open posture. When you press your hands and feet into the floor and lift your hips, the muscles between the shoulder blades activate while the glutes and core support the spine. Over time, this can help the body feel more upright, balanced, and confident during everyday movement. This exercise also strengthens the core in a very functional way. Core strength is not just about abdominal muscles. It is about stability. The core includes the muscles around the abdomen, lower back, and pelvis that help protect the spine during movement. When you lift your hips into the reverse tabletop position, your entire body must stabilize. The abdominal muscles support the spine while the glutes and back muscles maintain alignment. This type of strength helps the body feel more stable during daily activities like walking, carrying groceries, or standing up from a chair. Perhaps the most surprising benefit of the reverse tabletop exercise is that it helps the body relay natural movement patterns. Over time, the hips, shoulders, and spine may stop working together efficiently. The reverse tabletop gently restores this coordination by encouraging the body to move as one connected system again. Now, let's walk through how to perform the reverse tabletop exercise safely. Begin by sitting comfortably on the floor or on an exercise mat. Bend your knees and place your feet flat on the ground about hipwidth apart. Your feet should be close enough that your fingertips can almost reach your heels. Next, place your hands on the floor slightly behind your hips. Your fingers can point toward your feet or slightly outward depending on what feels comfortable for your wrists and shoulders. Take a slow breath in. As you exhale, press your hands and feet into the floor and begin lifting your hips upward. Raise your body until your torso forms a straight line from your shoulders to your knees, creating a tabletop shape. Keep your chest open and your shoulders stable. Engage your glutes and lightly tighten your abdominal muscles to support the spine. Hold this position for 3 to 5 seconds while breathing normally. Then slowly lower your hips back down with control. For most adults over 60, starting with six to eight repetitions is a great beginning. As your strength improves, you can gradually work toward 10 to 12 repetitions. Always listen to your body and move within a comfortable range. If lifting the hips fully feels difficult, you can start with a smaller lift and gradually increase as your strength improves. This video is intended for educational and wellness purposes only and is not a substitute for professional medical advice. If you have existing injuries or medical conditions, consider speaking with a health care professional before beginning a new exercise routine. Growing older does not mean becoming weaker. It simply means the body responds best to smart, consistent movement. Often the most powerful exercises are the ones that are simple and practiced regularly. Sometimes one small daily habit can create a big change over time. The reverse tabletop exercise is a perfect example of that by activating the hips, shoulders, and core together. It supports the body in moving with strength and confidence again. Now, we'd love to hear from you. Have you ever tried the reverse tabletop exercise before? Or is this the first time you're learning about it? Write your answer in the comments below and tell us your age and where you're watching from. Your story might inspire someone else in this community to start moving today. And if you found this video helpful, please like the video and subscribe to the channel for more simple exercises designed to help adults over 60 stay strong, balanced, and independent.

Along with boat rides in the Doki River, there are now many water activities like cliff jumping, kayaking, snokling or you can simply swim in the Doki River. But among all these, the living root bridges spread across the state are truly extraordinary. The Double Decker Root Bridges track starts from a small village named Tina. After reaching Tirana Village, you have to go down 3200 stairs to reach your final destination. Or when we activate the paddles, it enters that sequential sport mode , so to speak, where we have complete control of the revolutions and when to shift gears . Unless we push it to the limit, it will obviously shift up automatically, or if we do absolutely nothing and don't use the paddles, it goes back to showing " drive," it no longer displays the gear, and it's back in automatic sport mode, which I also think is cool, but I do want to keep that in mind. If you say, "I activated sport mode and I'm going to test it, expecting the greatest possible dynamism or the best engine response," that automatic sport mode isn't necessarily going to be as dynamic or aggressive as the sequential sport mode . So, I'll leave you with that extra tip. Here's also really cool: we can see the range we have with a tank that's not full, but it's more or less above 4/5 full. That gives us a range of 682 km. I think this speaks very well to the almost 800, or a little more than 800 km, total range this car can achieve, considering again that it's such a small car. Big, heavy, and with somewhat awkward aerodynamics, right? That much is true. But despite that, again, being a hybrid, it has excellent range, all the necessary torque for climbing hills and carrying any load, and it honestly feels like a much smaller car . It's super cool, and very smooth to drive. So it's perfect for adapting to any driving style you might throw at it. I also love these blind spot monitors that activate when you use the turn signals. They also help by telling you the engine RPM or speed depending on which side you activate them, because they're replacing the instrument cluster. And finally, the last comment I want to make is that if I take a slightly more aggressive turn, with a little more force, the car doesn't feel like a boat at all. I think the suspension is perfectly tuned and well-adjusted. It doesn't feel like the car is going to slide sideways, even when making turns on banked roads, which is something you sometimes expect, given how large the vehicle is, to behave like a boat. Similarly, when going over speed bumps, you barely feel them. The car also sounds perfectly tuned inside. I don't have the air conditioning, radio, or anything like that on, so I can hear the interior very clearly. It sounds perfectly tuned; there are no strange noises or anything like that, and the driving position does feel high, as it should be for an SUV in this category. In conclusion, the 2026 Santa Fe remains an impressive value proposition . Hyundai listened to the complaints about the transmission and gave us a more robust automatic gearbox for this turbo engine, which gives me much more peace of mind to recommend it, knowing that it's also a fully hybrid SUV with a fairly robust and decent electrical system . And yes, it's true that we lost a couple of quirky features like the V-shaped light in the glove compartment, and that rear design is still an acquired taste, but For the space, technology, and safety you get for the price, it's absolutely hard to ignore. This is a vehicle that here in Colombia costs 250 million pesos, and in the United States, you can buy it for approximately 45,000. So, friends, as I always say, I'm a small, honest creator trying to bring you the best content possible. If you found this video helpful or entertaining, the best way to support me is by giving it a like, subscribing to the channel, and telling me in the comments if you would buy this Santa Fe or if you perhaps prefer the previous design. I'm Daniel, this is Danmar Auto Reviews, and I'll see you in the next video. Take care.

- Why Is Bitcoin's Creator Still Anonymous? - Emerging Tech Insider: Kind: captions Language: en Why is Bitcoin's creator still anonymous? Imagine creating something that could change the world and then choosing to stay in the shadows. That's exactly what happened with Bitcoin. The person or group behind it goes by the name Satoshi Nakamoto, but their true identity remains a mystery. So, why would someone do that? Well, there are a few solid reasons rooted in privacy, security, and the core ideas of blockchain technology. First off, revealing who Satoshi Nakamoto is could put them at serious personal risk. Since Bitcoin controls a huge amount of digital wealth, if their identity was known, hackers or even governments might target them to seize assets or exert control. Privacy and safety are big concerns here. Think about how digital security works today. When you have a lot of valuable information online, keeping your identity private, helps prevent hacking attempts or threats. Nakamoto's anonymity acts like a shield, protecting them from potential harm. Another reason is legal and regulatory worries. Bitcoin challenged traditional financial systems and laws. If Nakamoto's identity was exposed, they could face legal actions or scrutiny from authorities trying to regulate or shut down the technology. Staying anonymous helps avoid these complications and keeps the focus on the technology itself. Decentralization is a key part of Bitcoin's design. It was built to be a currency without a central authority or single person in charge. If Nakamoto's identity was known, it might lead to centralization of influence, which goes against the entire idea of a communitydriven system. Anonymity keeps Bitcoin truly decentralized, owned by everyone and no one at the same time. There's also the aspect of neutrality. By remaining anonymous, Nakamoto prevents their personal reputation from influencing how Bitcoin is perceived or developed. It's about letting the technology speak for itself, not the charisma or background of its creator. This neutrality encourages open-source collaboration and community involvement, which are vital in blockchain and web 3 projects. Finally, Bitcoin was created as a social and technical experiment. The anonymity allows the technology to be judged on its merits rather than the personality behind it. It's a way to focus on the innovation and trustless systems that relying on cryptography and network consensus. All these reasons show that Nakamoto's anonymity is a strategic choice. It protects their safety, keeps the project decentralized, and emphasizes the principles of trust and community that are at the heart of blockchain and web 3. This mystery continues to fuel curiosity and discussion, making Bitcoin not just a digital currency, but also a symbol of privacy and independence in the digital age. [music]

- How Did Bitcoin's Creator Remain Anonymous? - Emerging Tech Insider: Kind: captions Language: en How did Bitcoin's creator remain as anonymous? Imagine creating a technology that could change the world but choosing to stay completely hidden. That's exactly what Satoshi Nakamoto did when inventing Bitcoin. So, how did this person or group keep their identity a secret? Let's break it down. First, Nakamoto used a pseudonym, which is a fake name. This name was only for online communication and never linked to any real person. They never shared personal details or revealed who they really were. Instead, all their messages and code releases came from this alias. Bitcoin's design also helps keep the creator anonymous. The blockchain is a public ledger that records every transaction, but it doesn't include any personal information about the users or the creator. This means Nakamoto's identity isn't tied to any transaction. They could send or receive bitcoins without revealing who they are. Nakamoto avoided public appearances or interviews. All communication was done through encrypted emails and online forums. They never posted pictures or gave any clues about their real identity. Network security played a big role, too. Nakamoto likely used privacy tools like the tour network which masks IP addresses and makes tracing difficult. This way they could communicate and work on Bitcoin without being tracked. Staying anonymous also protected Nakamoto from legal issues or government scrutiny. Bitcoin challenges traditional money systems, so revealing who made it could have brought legal trouble. Their anonymity also fits with Bitcoin's core idea of decentralization. No single person or group controls the system. Keeping Nakamoto hidden reinforced that the system was not dependent on one individual. It built trust in the technology itself, not in a person. From a broader tech view, Nakamuro's secrecy shows how cryptography and network design can protect identities. It's a good example of privacy in digital systems. It also inspired new privacy tools like zero knowledge proofs and mixing services which help users keep their transactions private while still being part of a transparent system. So Nakamoto's choice to stay anonymous wasn't just about hiding their identity. It was a way to strengthen the principles of decentralization, privacy, and trust that Bitcoin stands for.

- How the tech industry is manipulating you to buy more: Kind: captions Language: en When you think about Meta, you think about social media, but actually they're a surveillance advertising company. >> Senator, we run ads >> where they take all the data they can about you and sell it to advertisers. >> Data is going to be one of the most valuable things in tech companies that do nothing but offer you free email and a free search engine are going to be the biggest [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] companies in the world. And it's because of your data. >> The data has become the commodity in and of itself. The data is now what has value. You're followed also everywhere you go in person. Kids toys that are connected to the internet, robot vacuums, and even your car. >> What are all of your smart devices really doing? And what are they sending where? >> They had so much data on you and your emotional state and your life that they knew you'd be willing to pay more for that product. >> That they're going to start using some of these algorithms to price some of their tickets. They know you're in a tight spot and so you're going to be charged much more because of that. And when you said a minute ago at all times consumers have complete control of their data, that's just not true, is it? By the terms of your own agreement, that just is not true. >> Most of us have had one of those moments where it feels like your phone is reading your mind. Maybe you're chatting with a friend about a new lip gloss you wanted to try and then later that evening you got an ad for it. But while our phones are probably not listening to us, the truth might actually be worse. After all, they don't need to eased when they already have every morsel of our digital information. Enough to predict what we want even before we know what we want. Now, when I started my research for this video, I really just wanted to rant about smart devices and how annoying they've become. But boy howdy, was I ever taken down a rabbit hole because as I came to learn, the worst thing about smart devices isn't that they're unreliable and super finicky, but that they serve as the infrastructure for what's called surveillance capitalism. These devices, praised for being cutting edge, are actually just data- hungry little parasites quietly extracting every digital breadcrumb you leave behind and claiming it as corporate property to be tracked, distributed, and monetized. And I know most people don't care that their data is being collected, but the problem runs deeper than we think because it's no longer just about gathering information on us, but using that information against us to automate our behavior. But that's just the price we pay for free internet services, right? So today, we're going to peel back the glossy veneer of smart devices, talk about how they're getting all of our data, what they're doing with it, and how we can push back in a world where smart features are taking over. To start off, I'd like to share this quote with you. A man walks into a Minneapolis area Target angry about coupons his teenage daughter received for baby clothes and cribs. "Are you trying to encourage her to get pregnant?" he asks a store manager. except his daughter really was pregnant. Target had tuned a marketing prediction model so tightly that it could successfully tell what was happening inside her body before even the girl's family knew. While this is a less than ideal situation, it is something we're all familiar with, right? Not the teenage pregnancy, but the feeling like ads are reading our minds. I've been really coming around to the idea that our phones are listening to us cuz I think the personalized ads can be helpful. Like um literally this morning my friend was showing me her apple picking photos with her new boyfriend and I swear to God 10 minutes later I get an ad for a guy. How does my phone always know what I need? That's amazing. Like Apple was even recently in the news where they had to pay $95 million to settle a Siri listening lawsuit. But this digital stocking is starting to get out of hand. So to get to the bottom of it, let's first address what we already know and then take a dive into the deeper layers of the surveillance iceberg. We are living in a very strange era of capitalism where for the first time ever, we pretty much have unlimited free resources at our fingertips as long as you have a device that can connect to the internet. You can look up pretty much any piece of information, connect with anyone from your past, and now with AI, even create anything your mind desires. The information economy is booming and has been one of the most revolutionary moments for humanity. And this is the narrative we're told by the tech giants, right? That they're helping the world by connecting us all. >> We're a company that focuses on connecting people. >> From the pioneer of the search empire, Google, with their mission statement to organize the world's information and make it universally accessible and useful. How inspiring to the newer players in the game like OpenAI, who believes that AI should be available and beneficial to everyone, not just corporations, governments, or elites. But as we're all aware, they're not just doing this out of the goodness of their hearts. There's something in it for them as well. Because as they provide users with everything humanity has ever known, they're simultaneously helping themselves to a slice of the pie by collecting data from those very users. I requested all of the data that Amazon has on me. And here's what I found. I have two dots and one echo. These are all short voice clips. It's so scary. >> And that's just what the exchange is. As the saying goes, if you're not paying for the product, you are the product. We know this, but like I said, most people just don't care and sign up anyways because the benefits outweigh the drawbacks. The problem here is that this tracking goes far deeper than we think. So, to keep with the iceberg imagery, let's take a dip beneath the surface of the surveillance iceberg because Google has bigger plans than just saving our searches or helping out other drivers when you confirm or deny the state of construction on the road. In fact, pretty much every tech company, no matter what service they provide, is joining in on what Google started, the business of digitizing the real world. And nothing is exempt in this competition for surveillance revenues. Anything from smart doorbells, smart mattresses, smart scales, smart thermostats, and even smart electric toothbrushes. But what does the smart label really boil down to? Well, that it connects to the internet. Really? >> This guy bought this LG washing machine that was smart and Wi-Fi enabled. And one day he was looking at his internet consumption and realized that the washing machine was using three and a half gigabyte of data a day. >> What this allows companies to do is add complex capabilities to their products, but also opens up your world to their will. Because your smart TV isn't just smart by connecting all your streaming services in one place. It's screenshotting your shows, identifying them, sending that information to your TV manufacturer, and then selling the information to the highest bidder. But whether that's something you're okay with or not, it's just the reality of the smart industry. In July 2017, iRoot's autonomous vacuum cleaner, Roomba, made headlines when the company CEO told Rooers about its databased business strategy for the smart home. Starting with a new revenue stream derived from selling floor plans of customers homes scraped from the machine's new mapping capabilities, Angle indicated that iRoot could reach a deal to sell its maps to Google, Amazon, or Apple within the next 2 years. In preparation for this entry into surveillance competition, a camera, new sensors, and software had already been added to Roomba's premier line, enabling new functions, including the ability to build a map while tracking its own location. And this is a perfect example of companies tracking more than we know, because who would suspect their vacuum to be selling our data? But this happens because it's a combination of us being underinformed and feeling like we don't have an option anyway. When we buy a device, we don't read the hundreds of pages of terms and conditions. And even if we did, we still have to press accept unless we don't want the device to work. And the reason the smart industry has taken over electronics is for one reason, because it's so lucrative. Because we live in a capitalist society with open markets, companies are under constant pressure to show growth quarter after quarter. And the best way to do that is in the data collection business. Often, it's even their primary business model, not to sell you an expensive product, but to continuously extract data from you for profit. Some companies even go so far as making minimal, if any, profit off of the device because their main money maker is the data. >> Is it just me or smart TVs like super damn slow? >> No, it's not just you. They all suck. And here's why. Because it doesn't matter to them in the end. A good user experience doesn't sell them any more TVs. And so, they don't care. Anything from voice controlled speakers, Wi-Fi security cameras, TVs, kitchen appliances are probably generating and collecting data, even personal information, without you knowing. And as much as this sounds like a conspiracy theory, it unfortunately isn't. In 2015, privacy advocates discovered that the corporation's smart TVs were actually too smart, recording everything said in the vicinity of the TV. Please pass the salt. We're out of laundry detergent. I'm pregnant. Let's buy a new car. We're going to the movies now. I have a rare disease. She wants a divorce. He needs a new lunchbox. Do you love me? And sending all that talk to be transcribed by another market leader in voice recognition systems, Nuance Communications. Samsung acknowledges that the voice command aimed at triggering the TV's voice recognition capabilities are sent to a third party and adds, "Please be aware that if your spoken words include personal or other sensitive information, that information will be among the data captured and transmitted to a third party through your use of voice recognition. Super unsettling." Moving on to the next layer of our surveillance iceberg. It's not just that devices are taking the data that you provide it, but apps on your phone are talking to each other and exchanging information. It's common for tech companies to rely on thirdparty analytics and advertising tools in their smartphone apps, including some we use to manage smart devices. We tested the top Android free apps in Google Play and 96% of those popular free apps contained trackers. Companies that were different from the owners of the app. 87% of those sent data to Google and 68% sent data to Facebook. Apps that don't even use your location will gain access to it and track wherever you go just to add another revenue stream for them. >> My sister wears a Fitbit. When you think Fitbit, you don't think Google. >> No. >> But Google owns Fitbit. They they bought Fitbit so they could get all that physiological data 24 hours a day. Even the most innocent seeming applications such as weather, flashlight, ride sharing, and dating apps are full of tracking programs. This may be the final straw for the Weather Network app for me because you already know I struggle with their subscription service. And if they're also tracking me, I can't. But you, like most people, may still not care that your data is being collected because what do we have to hide, right? Like who cares if Google knows that I'm out of diapers and need to go pick up some more? You know, there's all this talk in the news about China having my data from Tik Tok and then maybe America has my data. Everybody wants my data. What are you going to do with it? Look at it. Giggle a little. Maybe get a little crush on me. Have it. I want you to. I don't give a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] But the problem is that there is still more to the story. This idea that if it's free, then you're the product isn't necessarily true because our collective understanding of the transaction going on is off. It's not just that these companies want to know what city you live in or that you take a cream in your coffee, but to build a complete digital profile of us as an avatar that they can analyze, predict, and monetize. What happens is that every time you open most apps, a series of transactions take place in an instant. Information about you is offered up for auction, and the data brokers around the world bid on a chance to place an ad in your view. The bids are placed on your profile which include standard information like your age, location, and gender, but also finer details like your personal interests, your desires, and your weaknesses. >> A lot of people, I was already very well aware that Facebook was taking my data and using it to make an ad profile to then sell to advertisers to make me buy things. But I feel like a lot of people don't understand the categories that they put you in are extremely specific and you can see them. I was in a category titled friends with women with birthdays in 7 to 30 days. I didn't even know that. And that has nothing to do with the women whose birthdays are in 1730 days. That has to do with me buying them a gift. >> All of these smart companies are in the business of mining every shrapnel of information they can pry off of you and add it to your avatar. Every time you set Google Maps to your workplace, download a fitness tracker, linger on anxiety related content, or look up real estate sites. This all adds information to your avatar about your routines, your body insecurities, your mental health state, and your income bracket. Inside servers, there is a little voodoo doll, and it's a model of you. It starts by not looking much like you. It's sort of a generic model of a human. But then they're collecting your click trails, everything you click on, everything you search for, and every detail of your life online. They're reassembling all that metadata you don't really think is meaningful so that the doll looks more and more like you. Why is Google Maps free? So, the voodoo doll can include the details of where you go every day. Why are Amazon Echo and Google Nest Hubs sold for as cheap as $30, far less than what they cost to make? So, they can gather more information so the voodoo doll can consist of not just what you search for on a screen, but what you say in your home. Then, when you show up, for example, on YouTube, they're waking that doll and testing out hundreds of thousands of videos against that doll, seeing what makes its arm twitch and move, so they know it's effective, and then they serve that to you. And this is something that feels pretty inescapable at this point because every year new products are being smartified and our dumb options are dwindling. The average American home has 17 internet connected devices silently observing their actions. And more than 40% of households have smart devices. They might have an Amazon Echo, a Roku TV, or perhaps a Google Nest thermostat and a few iPhones. And even for products that don't currently have tracking programs, if they're connected to the internet, then they can easily add tracking in at any time by just throwing up a pop-up on the screen and forcing you to accept if you want to continue using the product. So now I want to take a second to ask how is that allowed? You know, because if companies are able to just take what they want without any input from us, then our privacy as a whole is at risk here. So to actually understand what we're up against, it's important to know how we got here. Because surveillance capitalism is an evolution of our good old-fashioned capitalism brought to you primarily by Google. Like most tech companies, they started off with innocent intentions of trying to solve a problem, and they did do that. Google originally only collected data for its feedback loop of information in order to improve search. whatever users would click on would inform Google that that was the desired result for the inquiry. It was a symbiotic relationship where search needed people and people needed search. There was a slight problem though and that was that giving away a service for free doesn't make any money. So if Google didn't pivot from just an information resource, it would have died out. The inevitable shift did eventually come when Google started taking a little extra data off the top from its users to sell as behavioral surplus and create a new revenue stream, targeted ads. But this was just the cornerstone of what would eventually become surveillance capitalism. And Google isn't the only bad guy here. Everyone jumped on this train following their success. Like Meta, who we are backtoback bashing in videos, I guess, but they are pretty um horrible. So So don't feel bad about it. >> Still about bringing people together. To clarify, not because of regulation or law, but because of user resistance. But even still, not much has changed because Meta still tracks users via invisible code and embedded social widgets. What this looks like is that if you just visit a website that has a Facebook share or like button, your data can be sent to Meta. Even if you don't click the button, you're not logged into Facebook or don't even have a Facebook account. Facebook uses a number of software tools to do this tracking. Invisible code that's dropped onto the other websites that allows the site and Facebook to track users activity. All while countless other companies rush to join the ride. And as unnerving as all of this is, we haven't even gotten to the worst part yet. Because now we know that we're kind of so and out on our own here. But most importantly, we need to talk about how these companies use our digital avatars to influence what we see, think, and do. [Music] So, like I mentioned earlier, the idea that we are the product is flawed because in reality, we're more like the raw material that these companies mine in the first stage of a business model designed to understand what we think, feel, and do. But the real endgame is to use that information to shape us into the most profitable resource of all, a predictable producer of purchases. The algorithm isn't something you curate, it's curating you. It is a common belief that we are somewhat in control of our feeds by consciously liking or following or sharing certain content. In reality, these algorithmdriven platforms are subtly shaping our behaviors and perceptions by using algorithms that prioritize engagement over everything else. And their goal is not just to give you what you want, but to actually leave you wanting more. >> It's not about showing someone the right ad. You have to show it at the right place and time with the language and imagery calibrated for precise effect. You have to lead people through the physical world, making them show up at the sponsored pop-up store or vote for the preferred candidate. Armed with a veritable real-time feed of a user's thoughts and feelings, companies are beginning to practice just this kind of coercion, which is why you might see makeup ads before a Friday evening out, or why inducements from a personal injury lawyer might pop up on your phone as you sit in the hospital waiting room. when we want things, health information, travel schedules, a date. It's also when we're most vulnerable. When intimate data yields themselves for corporate capture. We live in a new realm of capitalism where companies can infer thoughts, feelings, and intentions into people in the name of directing them to whatever is best for business. All they have to do is make sophisticated nudges based on who we are, what we want, and what we're most likely to do. What this actually looks like is apps knowing when you're most vulnerable or bored and pushing notifications accordingly. If they know when you're at your weakest, they can coax you into retail therapy because your guard is down. If they know you're about to start a new adventure in life, like having a baby, to circle back to the example we started the video off with, then they can sell you products that act as solutions to problems you didn't even know you had. Like ads for medicinal drops for collic, bouncers for fussy babies, or blackout curtains for sleepless nights. Even if those aren't things that your child actually needs, they just feed into your insecurities of the unknown future of parenthood. Now, to be clear, influencing what people buy isn't new. That's basically just what marketing is. But what we're dealing with now is an entirely different playing field. Today, we are willingly handing over our desires and vulnerabilities to the very people trying to sell to us. These companies don't just collect your data to sell to you. They sell the ability to influence you. And the more they can influence you, the more predictable you become, making it even that much easier to influence you. Again, the genius and the danger of the system lies in its market logic. The more our actual behavior conforms to these data-driven predictions, the more valuable those data become. This creates a feedback loop where the system refineses itself, making us more visible and predictable, which further enhances its economic power. Zubof warns of the erosion of personal choice and free will as our lives become more algorithmically managed. Our autonomy begins to diminish. So what this means is that companies can use behavior modification to manipulate users into taking actions and most importantly making purchases that we otherwise wouldn't have. All with the goal of creating the perfect shopper. Someone whose desires aren't even their own anymore because they've accepted the manufactured desires that machines have chosen for us. It's not so much that the algorithm knows what we want before we do, but that they've already rewired our brain to make us want what it's about to serve to us. And no, it doesn't stop there, because surveillance capitalism has continued to evolve into surveillance pricing, a concept that's been gathering attention over the past year. While dynamic pricing isn't new either. You know, gas prices rise before long weekends or in the mornings on your way to work, it is however changing to now include the variable of personal information. Imagine entering a supermarket whose shelves might someday adjust prices dynamically as you traverse the aisles. Perhaps the shelves themselves will vanish, replaced by screens that display in high resolution, the products the store deems most compatible with your derived needs and desires. Maybe you'd revolt, but probably you'd just check out. While this does sound like a Black Mirror dystopian future, it is the reality we're facing now. Companies are taking on the strategy of changing their prices based on your personal information like location, browsing history, and your spending habits. If they know it's something you want, then prices go up. We saw this with Delta Airlines this past month, sharing that they will be using dynamic pricing based on an AI platform that uses data, allegedly personal data, to set those prices. And then we have Uber, who's been in the game for a long time, who increases prices if they pick you up from an airport or an expensive hotel. Uber says we know more about driver and rider behavior so we can figure out who is willing to pay more as a passenger or accept less as a driver. Since implementing upfront pricing, Uber has increased rider prices, has cut driver pay, has increased its take rates, and of course has greatly improved its cash flow. The danger this imposes is one that you can probably guess it takes away our autonomy as it nudges us towards desires that align more with the companies profiling us rather than our own. But what's interesting is that it has been a slow burn to get here. It's not like we woke up one day and discovered that all our personal information had been taken. Taking a little here and a little there, but never enough at once to spark real outrage or meaningful push back. The trick has been a gradual roll out, a quiet erosion of privacy paired with the steady loss of autonomy. And now we've grown so used to it that we barely question it. We grow numb to these incursions and the ways in which they devour our lives. That this no longer seems astonishing to us or perhaps even worthy of note is evidence of the profound psychic numbing that has innered us to a bold and unprecedented shift in capitalist methods. We succumb to the drum beatat of inevitability. Opposition is fleeting like the sharp yelp of pain when the nova needle first pierces the flesh before numbness sets in. - Is Your Phone Listening To You?: Kind: captions Language: en You are having a coffee with a close friend and bring up how you need a new bathing suit Then BAM all of a sudden there are ads on your phone for bathing suits. Our smartphones are with us at all times, but are companies and governments using them to spy on us? You may be suspicious for a good reason. A New York Times piece uncovered that thousands of apps, including gaming apps for kids are equipped with the software Alfonso. Alfonso uses your smartphone microphone to listen to the ads that you are watching on TV. It uses audio signals to collect TV viewing data in order for advertisers to personally promote things to you online. Alfonso "claims" to not record human speech. Facebook has created a device called Pixel, which is designed as a way to continue tracking you online even after you've left a website. By dropping a small piece of data called a cookie, your device will now remember information, like how long you linger on a certain item, and whether you added anything to your shopping cart, but didn't purchase it. Millions of websites use Facebook Pixel, which is why the hat you didn't end up buying follows you around Facebook forever But sites like Facebook even follow you offline and have been known to purchase information and create a database of over 52,000 different attributes about you, for example, Facebook buys data from supermarket loyalty programs. So if you have a points card with a grocery store Facebook may be paying to understand your purchasing patterns Potentially learning about what you eat or even what type of toothpaste you use. So, your phone can listen to you, but it can also follow you as well. Every time you receive a text or use data to load an app, companies can log and retain your location based on the cell tower and cellular antenna you connect it to. The precision of location has increased over time and get paint a detailed picture of where you go. So, your phone may start sending you ads for swimsuits because you were just physically in or near a surf shop. These companies will claim they aren't doing anything you haven't agreed to as you have accepted the privacy policy that allows the collection of your information. And did you read the fine print? Probably not. A study presented 543 participants with a fake app called Name Drop to see how many would read the privacy policy and terms of service. 74% skipped reading the agreement all together, and those who did read it didn't do a great job. 98% percent of participants missed the gotcha clauses, which included sharing all your data with the NSA, your employer, and agreeing to provide your firstborn child as payment And what about the rise of smart speakers like Google Home and Amazon Alexa? Which are designed to listen to you at all times. Though, they are supposed to only record your voice when giving prompts like "Ok, Google" Recently a couple found that Amazon's Alexa recorded a private conversation and then randomly sent it to a friend These are deemed unfortunate glitches. But what if someone gained access to them as Wi-Fi networks and mobile internet devices are easier to hack than you might think? Your typical wireless internet information is kept confidential by being converted from a readable state to nonsense at the access point and then converted back to a readable state when you use the correct Wi-Fi password from your computer. When Internet moved from physically wired networks to wireless technology it became easier to hack and less secure because the encryption schemes are relatively simple For example, Your webcam can be hacked by creating a remote access tool which is the digital equivalent of someone adding an unlocked window to your home without you even noticing. Also, the FBI Agent meme isn't far from the truth, as the FBI is known to use phishing, which is slipping a link into a message that is labeled in a misleading way... when you hit the link your device connects to the FBI's computer downloading malware, which is then able to covertly spy on you. Long story short, your phone is capable of listening to you, following you, and even watching you. It has features like an automatic kill switch, browser proxy extension, ad blocking, and many more. - Why BTS Solo Stans Need To Go: Kind: captions Language: en Solo stans have been a hot topic of&nbsp; conversation for years, and still are&nbsp;today.&nbsp; There have been numerous debates on the&nbsp; true nature of their existence and whether or&nbsp; not we should allow them to thrive in&nbsp; our communities. But, strangely enough,&nbsp;&nbsp; not many have done an in-depth discussion&nbsp; about it on YouTube. Let's fix that, shall we? Hi. My name is Adaeze, Daezy for short, and this is&nbsp; my channel, Triple A Thoughts, where my videos will be&nbsp;&nbsp; BTS-focused and way longer than any Twitter thread of mine can do justice. Yes, I am still calling it Twitter. Elon Musk can choke on that. Speaking of Twitter, you can also follow my page there. Aside from posting links to my videos, I'll also be&nbsp; posting important updates regarding my channel and just talking about BTS in general. So, follow&nbsp;if you're interested. But with all that self-promo out of the way, let's get back to the topic at&nbsp;hand, solo stans. Some of you who are watching this video might be new to BTS or even K-pop as&nbsp;a whole and wonder: what exactly is a solo stan? According to Urban Dictionary, the definition of&nbsp; a solo stan is when you like only one person in a group. the group, in this case, referring to a&nbsp; K-Pop group. In the context of BTS, a BTS solo stan would be someone who likes and supports only&nbsp; one member of BTS. There's also the term 'diet solo', which is defined as someone who does not self-identify as a solo stan just yet, but is already on their way to becoming one. Solo stans&nbsp;and diet solo stans also fall under the category of being a 'manti', which is a combination of the&nbsp;words 'manager' and 'anti'. The term 'manti' refers to somebody who thinks they know how to manage BTS better than BTS's own label and BTS themselves. They think their opinion is the only correct one and that anyone who disagrees with them do not have BTS's best interests at heart. So, it's one of those cases where not every manti is a solo stan or a diet solo, but every solo stan and diet solo is a manti. There are multiple variations of BTS solo stans, but regardless of whether you like only one, two, or three members, the main focus of being a solo stan is that you are not 'OT7'. Meaning, you don't love all seven members. "But what harm does not being OT7 do?" you might ask. "As long as they don't disrespect the other members, just supporting one or a few of them should be okay, right?" You're not alone in thinking that. There are many people in the Kpop community who believe that solo stans are not problematic as long as they don't disrespect&nbsp; the other members of any given group. Because&nbsp;&nbsp; once you start going out of your way to drag&nbsp; the other members, that's when you become an&nbsp;&nbsp; 'akgae'. Once more according, to Urban Dictionary, an akgae is an abbreviation of the Korean words&nbsp;&nbsp; 'akseong geinpaen', which translates to 'malicious individual fan'. They only love and support one or a few members of a group, just like solo stands do, but akgaes go the&nbsp;extra mile and send hate towards the members they&nbsp;&nbsp; don't like, generally because they believe their&nbsp; favorite member (or favorite members) are the only&nbsp;&nbsp; talented ones in the group. Within this definition,&nbsp; they even make the distinction that solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; are not equal to akgaes, and here, ladies and gentlemen,&nbsp;is where the problem lies. This distinction, I feel&nbsp;&nbsp; is what allows people to justify the existence&nbsp; of solo stan communities. People, especially solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stans take this distinction and milk it for all&nbsp;it's worth, convincing people that it's okay to be a&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stan as long as they don't disrespect the&nbsp; other members. But people continuously fail to&nbsp;&nbsp; ask one crucial question: What happens when solo&nbsp;stans disrespect the member that they're a fan of? There are several issues I want to discuss when it&nbsp; comes to the harm that solo stans inflict on the&nbsp;&nbsp; fandom, but quick disclaimer before I start, just&nbsp; so this video doesn't get too long, I will only be&nbsp;&nbsp; picking a handful of popular incidents to discuss.&nbsp; If I don't touch on a specific issue or make too&nbsp;&nbsp; much mention of a specific member's solo stans, that&nbsp;doesn't mean I'm not aware of them or think that they don't cause as much trouble, but I simply just&nbsp; do not have the time. I encourage you to do some&nbsp;&nbsp; digging for yourself once this video is finished,&nbsp; there's plenty of incidents for you to look at. Or,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you know of any that I haven't mentioned that&nbsp; you'd like to talk about, you can also drop it in&nbsp;&nbsp; the comments. Without further ado, let's get into&nbsp; it. The first issue I have with solo stans is the&nbsp;&nbsp; constant victimization of their favorite member.&nbsp; In the mind of a solo stan, their favorite member&nbsp;&nbsp; is always the underdog. According to them, the&nbsp; fandom never appreciates their favorite member&nbsp;&nbsp; the way they should be and they believe their&nbsp; favorite member is constantly given the short&nbsp;&nbsp; end of the stick by the other members, their label,&nbsp; or both. And the craziest thing is that every solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stan and every diet solo stan will say the exact same&nbsp;thing about their favorite member: Why is it always&nbsp;him? "It's so annoying, really. Why is it always him?&nbsp; What's up? Why always Hoseok, Big Hit? They're trying&nbsp;to tell us something."&nbsp; "Why is it always him? Why are&nbsp; Yoongi's parts always cut?" "Why is it always Jin, huh? BTS has seven members, don't leave him out." "Not gonna lie, but Joon didn't get much recognition with his&nbsp;latest collab while Suga's interlude got so much&nbsp;attention. Why is it always him?" "Why does it always&nbsp;have to be him? He's the biggest OT7 enthusiast out&nbsp;there, no one deserves this mistreatment, especially&nbsp;&nbsp; this precious human being right here. Why always&nbsp; Tae? I know, really getting sick of it, really."&nbsp;&nbsp; "So, can someone tell me why it's always Jungkook who&nbsp;gets treated like this in the fandom? People here&nbsp;&nbsp; will glad defend someone who mocks his talent&nbsp; rather than supporting him." "I'm so annoyed. Why is&nbsp;it always Jimin? Why do people always discredit his&nbsp;achievements? Why only him?" And this is usually how&nbsp;the descent down the solo stan pipeline begins. Someone makes an inaccurate observation that their&nbsp;&nbsp; favorite member is always left out or treated&nbsp; differently than the other members, which leads to&nbsp; those who have been exposed to this observation to&nbsp;root for that member more, to make up for whatever&nbsp;&nbsp; support is believed to be lacking from the fandom,&nbsp; the company, or the rest of the group. In doing so,&nbsp;&nbsp; they are exposed to more narratives that paint&nbsp; this member as mistreated or underappreciated.&nbsp; And while their sympathy for that member grows,&nbsp; resentment towards those mistreating that member grows alongside it until the day they decide to&nbsp; fully distance themselves from the fandom and&nbsp;&nbsp; integrate into the solo stan community. Within&nbsp; that community, people continue to uphold the&nbsp;&nbsp; idea that solo stans are the only ones who truly&nbsp; love and support their favorite member, and that&nbsp;&nbsp; everyone else is simply a fake fan. And, like I&nbsp; just said, every solo stan for every member of BTS&nbsp;&nbsp; wholeheartedly believes that their favorite member&nbsp; is being mistreated, so with beliefs like that, you&nbsp;&nbsp; naturally end up with tweets like these: "Jin only has Seokjinnies, and that's the truth. Don't blame us&nbsp;if we become more protective by calling out these&nbsp;so-called OT7 accounts, knowing the fact that Jin&nbsp; didn't get the same support and energy from them." "We can safely say that Taehyung only has KTHs!" "Double&nbsp;your focus on Jimin, 'cause Jimin only has PJMs." "Honestly, I&nbsp;can understand that. It's like J-Hope only has Hopeworldians. Fake OT7 stans will use him for their&nbsp;thirst tweets when he walks shirtless around the&nbsp;airport." "Namjoon only has us Moonchildren and&nbsp; it SHOWS." "Jungkook only has us JJKs. The fandom, the&nbsp;shippers, no one really cares about him." "Yoongi only&nbsp;has the MYGs watching his back because ARMYs will&nbsp;stab him if they get the chance." &nbsp;&nbsp; This supplements my argument that not only do solo stans victimize their favorite member, but they operate under&nbsp; the assumption that they are the only support&nbsp;&nbsp; system that their favorite member has. Seeing&nbsp; one of these tweets from one solo stan of a&nbsp;&nbsp; particular member may be convincing in isolation,&nbsp; but putting these tweets side-by-side with solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stans of other members makes you realize how&nbsp; ridiculous all of this is. If every solo stan&nbsp;&nbsp; believes that their favorite member is a victim,&nbsp; then it's more likely that all of them are simply&nbsp;&nbsp; just seeing what they want to see, and that nobody&nbsp; from BTS is actually being mistreated. And honestly,&nbsp;&nbsp; I could move on to the next point, but I think&nbsp; seeing examples of victimization in action might&nbsp;&nbsp; help cement this idea even further. One example&nbsp; I can think of is the way that people have been&nbsp;&nbsp; handling the release and promotion of BTS members' individual albums. For context, on June 14th, 2022,&nbsp;&nbsp; BTS announced through their Bangtan Dinner&nbsp; Party video that they would be taking a break&nbsp;&nbsp; from group releases to focus on solo activities&nbsp; and that each member will be releasing their own&nbsp;&nbsp; solo projects before reconvening as a group at&nbsp; a then unspecified time, which we now know to be&nbsp;&nbsp; 2025 through later statements. Shortly after this&nbsp; announcement, BTS's label, Big Hit Music, revealed&nbsp;&nbsp; that J-Hope would be the first member to release&nbsp; a solo album called Jack In The Box on July 15th,&nbsp;&nbsp; 2022. With that announcement came the obvious&nbsp; excitement from ARMYs, eager to get new music&nbsp;&nbsp; so soon after BTS's comeback with their Proof&nbsp; album, but all of this came to a screeching halt&nbsp;&nbsp; when it was announced that J-Hope's physical album&nbsp;would not include a CD, but instead a QR code that&nbsp;&nbsp; would link you to listen to his album digitally on&nbsp; the Weverse Albums app. Aside from the fact that a&nbsp;&nbsp; CD-less album upset people who are avid collectors&nbsp;of BTS merch, and so wouldn't be able to display&nbsp;&nbsp; J-Hope's album like other BTS albums they might&nbsp; own, another subset of people was upset because&nbsp;&nbsp; J-Hope's album being CD-less also meant that none of&nbsp;his album sales would count towards the Billboard 200&nbsp;&nbsp; in the US. While sales aren't the only way&nbsp; to climb the charts, they are still a pretty&nbsp;&nbsp; important factor, and with a fandom as big&nbsp; as ours, some people thought that Big Hit was&nbsp;&nbsp; wasting a big opportunity for J-Hope to have a&nbsp; high-ranking chart position on the Billboard 200&nbsp;&nbsp; in the first tracking week of his album. More&nbsp; fuel was added to the fire when, amidst all this&nbsp;&nbsp; discourse, came a popular account within the K-pop&nbsp;sphere known as TMIKpop, an account dedicated to&nbsp;&nbsp; summarizing and sharing articles about the latest&nbsp; news within the K-pop industry. On July 6, 2022,&nbsp;&nbsp; TMIKpop posted a Twitter thread summarizing some&nbsp;articles written about environmentally wasteful&nbsp;&nbsp; practices of some K-pop fans, where they will&nbsp; buy albums en masse to set records, qualify to&nbsp;&nbsp; enter fan-signs and collect photo cards, but then&nbsp; dump these albums once their goals had been met.&nbsp;&nbsp; These articles also talked about some of the steps&nbsp; some K-pop entertainment companies were taken to&nbsp;&nbsp; tackle these issues, one of these steps being HYBE,&nbsp;Big Hit's parent company, appointing CEO of Green Fund&nbsp;&nbsp; and Environmental Social Governance expert, Lee&nbsp; Mi-Kyung as a new board member.
