Within these articles,&nbsp;&nbsp; it was stated that HYBE planned to establish&nbsp; a complete ESG strategy under her guidance,&nbsp;&nbsp; the journalists of these articles implying that&nbsp; J-Hope's CD-less album was part of this strategy.&nbsp;&nbsp; While some people didn't care all too much, they&nbsp; were quickly overshadowed by those who considered&nbsp;&nbsp; this blatant sabotage by HYBE, claiming that the&nbsp; company was selfishly using J-Hope as a 'guinea&nbsp;&nbsp; pig' for their ESG strategy instead of giving&nbsp; them a proper album that would both be bought by&nbsp;&nbsp; more fans for collection purposes, and actually&nbsp; count towards the Billboard charts. There were&nbsp;&nbsp; also weird rumors going around at some point of J-Hope's album being an NFT as well, which was just&nbsp;&nbsp; untrue, and I'm still not quite sure who started&nbsp; that narrative. Either way, the damage was done&nbsp;&nbsp; and people were furious, especially solo stans and&nbsp; their diet counterparts. For weeks up until his&nbsp;&nbsp; album release, the weeks after his album release,&nbsp; and arguably even present-day, people are still&nbsp;&nbsp; saying that HYBE has ruined J-Hope's official solo&nbsp; debut as an artist because of their need to test&nbsp;&nbsp; their ESG strategy on J-Hope instead of testing&nbsp; it on other groups under the label whose album&nbsp;&nbsp; production put out way more waste than BTS's.&nbsp; And for the longest time, the general consensus&nbsp;&nbsp; was that J-Hope did not agree for his album to be&nbsp; CD-less and that this decision was made against his&nbsp;&nbsp; will, which is where the whole victimization thing&nbsp; comes in. But what has J-Hope himself said about&nbsp;&nbsp; the CD-less album? On July 19th, 2023, J-Hope appeared as a guest on Suchwita, BTS member Suga's drinking&nbsp;&nbsp; show. And when talking about Jack In The Box,&nbsp; he said, "I started out with a mixtape concept, so I&nbsp;&nbsp; worked on that in January. And since the idea was&nbsp; to do a mixtape, I thought I'd release a Weverse&nbsp;&nbsp; album or an LP just to commemorate the occasion. But like you said, there was a bigger need for a&nbsp;&nbsp; physical album, a full album, than I expected. The&nbsp; fans wanted it so much, and you and Jimin would also&nbsp;&nbsp; be releasing solo albums, so the fact that I didn't&nbsp; have a physical album did bother me a little, and I&nbsp;&nbsp; also felt really sorry about that to the fans, so I&nbsp; thought I should make one. I took new photos for it&nbsp;&nbsp; and renewed everything. It's going to be a proper&nbsp; album, the kind that the fans wanted, that's going&nbsp;&nbsp; to be released for the first anniversary of Jack&nbsp; In The Box." There you have it. We have J-Hope saying&nbsp;&nbsp; out of his own mouth that it was his decision for&nbsp; Jack In The Box to originally not have a physical&nbsp;&nbsp; CD, since he was going for more of a mixtape kind&nbsp; of concept. But since so many people were unhappy&nbsp;&nbsp; at the idea of a CD-less album, he felt bad for&nbsp; ARMYs and eventually ended up re-releasing&nbsp;&nbsp; the album with a physical CD version that also&nbsp; contained added instrumental and live versions of&nbsp;&nbsp; existing tracks. This is further backed up in the&nbsp; 'thanks to' message that J-Hope put in the Jack In The&nbsp;&nbsp; Box: Hope Edition physical album. It's a very long&nbsp; yet important message, so I urge you to pause this&nbsp;&nbsp; video and read it in full before continuing, but to&nbsp; summarize what J-Hope has said in this note, he felt&nbsp;&nbsp; sorry that it took so long for a physical CD to&nbsp; be released and that he could have left Jack In&nbsp;&nbsp; The Box as it originally was, but after hearing&nbsp; feedback, he didn't want it to become a project&nbsp;&nbsp; that we would grow to resent because of his&nbsp; original intention to leave the album CD-less. I'm&nbsp;&nbsp; going to be frank, I find it absolutely appalling&nbsp; that solo stans and diet solos harassed Big Hit&nbsp;&nbsp; about a CD release so much that J-Hope felt the&nbsp; need to not only apologize for his own creative&nbsp;&nbsp; decisions, but chalk it up to his own unawareness&nbsp; of what people really wanted. This behavior is&nbsp;&nbsp; unacceptable, and the message it sends to BTS is&nbsp; that there actually is a limit to how creative&nbsp;&nbsp; they're allowed to be before people who claim to&nbsp; be their fans start throwing temper tantrums. As&nbsp;&nbsp; long as it doesn't harm anyone, no artist should&nbsp; ever have to apologize for making their art the&nbsp;&nbsp; way they want to make it, and this is just one&nbsp; example out of the many claims of victimization.&nbsp;&nbsp; With each new member that has dropped solo work, there have been more solo stans claiming that&nbsp;&nbsp; their favorite member is being sabotaged, like RM, for example. On December 2nd, 2022, RM dropped his&nbsp;&nbsp; solo album, Indigo, it had been teased since&nbsp; November 10th, the announcement first being&nbsp;&nbsp; posted on on RM's personal Instagram, and there&nbsp; were many things posted by both RM and Big Hit&nbsp;&nbsp; to build up hype. But not everyone was satisfied&nbsp; with what was being released. November 21st, 2022,&nbsp;&nbsp; a week and a half before Indigo was released, this&nbsp; account tweeted, "Hope y'all don't get me wrong, but&nbsp;&nbsp; at this point, the disregard for his album has&nbsp; reached the ridiculous point. 10 days to release&nbsp;&nbsp; the album and we don't even have the title of the&nbsp; main track, this is crazy." And at surface level, one&nbsp;&nbsp; might agree with this, but in my humble opinion,&nbsp; this could only be considered ridiculous if it&nbsp;&nbsp; was atypical of RM's usual promotional style. The&nbsp; truth is RM actually has a history of being lowkey&nbsp;&nbsp; when it comes to solo projects. Back in 2018, the&nbsp; only heads-up we received about the release of&nbsp;&nbsp; RM second mixtape, mono, was a brief announcement&nbsp;he made at the end of a concert in Paris only two&nbsp;&nbsp; days before it was supposed to drop, followed by a&nbsp; post that same night on Big Hit's Twitter page with&nbsp;&nbsp; a picture of the mixtape's cover art and a full&nbsp; track list. No other posts about it came until two&nbsp;&nbsp; days later, when Big Hit had posted the official links&nbsp;to listen to the mixtape on streaming platforms&nbsp;&nbsp; and watch the Forever Rain music video on YouTube, and only a couple other things were released, such&nbsp;&nbsp; as reposted articles about mono and the lyric&nbsp; videos for two of mono's b-sides. That was pretty&nbsp;&nbsp; much it. Fast forward to 2022, RM did a live stream&nbsp; on Weverse on July 21st where, when talking about his&nbsp;&nbsp; upcoming album, he had this to say: "I think I'm&nbsp; going to go on a bit of a different path from&nbsp;&nbsp; J-Hope while doing fun things... I think I'm going to&nbsp; go in my own character and kind of quiet. Anyway, I&nbsp;&nbsp; plan on getting involved a lot, so I want to show&nbsp; you great sides." So, from both of these instances,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can see that RM is the type of person to&nbsp; prefer less extravagant promotion, but because&nbsp;&nbsp; RM hasn't dropped the name of his title track&nbsp; yet, people think that Big Hit is neglecting the&nbsp;&nbsp; promotion of his album, even though RM himself&nbsp; had cut things way closer in the past. This&nbsp;&nbsp; situation got even stickier with this account's&nbsp; follow-up tweet two hours later, after receiving very&nbsp;&nbsp; swift backlash, when they said, "Never said that I&nbsp; disagreed with anything he said or that I didn't&nbsp;&nbsp; trust him. I just talked about not thinking&nbsp; that it was right that the promotion of his&nbsp;&nbsp; album was up to him. Okay, I see that y'all don't&nbsp; agree with me, don't need to tell me to k(ill) myself&nbsp;&nbsp; or treat me like a traitor." Before I pick apart&nbsp; this response, I want to preface this by saying&nbsp;&nbsp; that under no circumstances do I, or should anyone, condone threats of any kind, especially those that&nbsp;&nbsp; encourage self-harm. Although we can agree that&nbsp; death threats are wrong while also acknowledging&nbsp;&nbsp; that what this account said about Indigo is still&nbsp; a piss-poor take. And on top of that, this account&nbsp;&nbsp; has not acknowledged the loads of valid criticism&nbsp; that they received on their original tweet. But&nbsp;&nbsp; let's talk about the first part of this response.&nbsp; You'll notice from the wording that despite&nbsp;&nbsp; the fact that they seemingly understand that&nbsp; this promotional style was RM's decision, they&nbsp;&nbsp; still don't think it should be left up to him. This&nbsp; naturally sparked even more outrage from ARMYs.&nbsp;&nbsp; A bit later, an ARMY shared a private message that&nbsp; they had received from that same account, putting&nbsp;&nbsp; them on blast for wanting to resolve their "public&nbsp; actions in private". Within this private message, the&nbsp;&nbsp; account attempts to elaborate what they meant&nbsp; in their response tweet. They said that they&nbsp;&nbsp; weren't trying to say that they didn't want RM&nbsp; to promote his own album, but that they think it's&nbsp;&nbsp; unfair that it was only left up to him since,&nbsp; quote and unquote, "everything we know about Indigo&nbsp;&nbsp; comes solely from Namjoon. The company doesn't even&nbsp;post support. Not only is this victimizing, but this&nbsp;&nbsp; is also just factually incorrect. From the day RM&nbsp; announced this album on November 10th to the day&nbsp;&nbsp; this account tweeted on November 21st, both the&nbsp; general Big Hit Twitter page and the BTS-exclusive&nbsp;&nbsp; Big Hit Twitter page had been taking turns&nbsp; tweeting information such as the actual album&nbsp;&nbsp; announcement, links to pre-order the physical album, and reposted articles about Indigo's release.&nbsp;&nbsp; And from November 24th, both of these accounts posted&nbsp;promotional tweets almost every day up to Indigo's&nbsp;&nbsp; release like photo sketches, interview trailers,&nbsp; the album tracklist, and the music video teaser&nbsp;&nbsp; for the title track, amongst other things. And even&nbsp; after Indigo dropped, more promotional content was&nbsp;&nbsp; released by Big Hit such as live shows, streaming&nbsp; parties, bonus music videos, and performances across&nbsp;&nbsp; multiple different platforms. In short, the problem&nbsp; wasn't that there was no promotion for Indigo.&nbsp;&nbsp; The real problem was that this person, and others like&nbsp;them, were too impatient. They believed that RM's&nbsp; promotional style wasn't good enough and projected&nbsp;that dislike onto RM himself, fabricating this lie&nbsp;&nbsp; that there was a disconnect between RM&nbsp; and Big Hit in terms of how Indigo's promotion was&nbsp; supposed to be handled, simply because they were&nbsp; afraid that this supposed lack of promotion would&nbsp;&nbsp; lead to poor metrics. I think these tweets sum up&nbsp; exactly what I'm thinking. "The crazy part about all&nbsp;&nbsp; this discourse over promo is that it all boils&nbsp; down to some of y'all's obsession with numbers.&nbsp;&nbsp; You're not asking for promo for yourself, you know&nbsp; something is coming. You're asking because you fear&nbsp;&nbsp; the release not making the numbers you personally&nbsp; wanted it to make because of exposure. Y'all let stats&nbsp;&nbsp; that have nothing to do with the artistic vision&nbsp; impact how you personally consume and enjoy a&nbsp;&nbsp; release to the point of questioning their methods&nbsp; and denying their artistic autonomy because you're&nbsp;&nbsp; insecure about how the release may go because of&nbsp; lack of promo, and that's annoying." This really hit&nbsp;&nbsp; the nail on the head in my opinion, especially&nbsp; after hearing what Namjoon had to say about&nbsp;&nbsp; charting in general for his album. On December&nbsp; 5th, 2022, the first episode of Suchwita was posted to&nbsp;&nbsp; BTS's official YouTube channel BANGTANTV with RM as a guest. At some point during the episode, Suga asked RM if he felt pressured thinking about where&nbsp; Indigo might place on the charts, and he had this&nbsp;&nbsp; to say. "Chart performance is important, there's no&nbsp; denying that at all. BTS and RM are my everything,&nbsp;&nbsp; so even if I release an album, I don't think my&nbsp; name could ever get bigger than BTS. So for my&nbsp;&nbsp; music, I'm not indifferent to the charts, but I wanted to be completely free. But if I say I don't care&nbsp;&nbsp; about rankings, that would be a lie. I do care, but&nbsp; since my purpose or goal isn't to top the charts,&nbsp;&nbsp; I wanted to do my best where I could. I really&nbsp; don't mind much about rankings." This was further&nbsp;&nbsp; solidified when during RM's live performance of&nbsp; Indigo at Seoul Rolling Hall that same day, he said,&nbsp;&nbsp; "You all often say to me, 'do everything you want&nbsp; to do, Kim Namjoon.' This time, I really did everything&nbsp;&nbsp; I wanted to. Not thinking about rankings in the&nbsp; US or Korea, things like that. Look at me now, too,&nbsp;&nbsp; I'm doing what I want to. I'm doing a concert like&nbsp; this after nearly ten years, and I really wanted to&nbsp;&nbsp; do one." And before anyone says anything, don't get&nbsp;me wrong. RM saying that he doesn't really mind&nbsp;&nbsp; the rankings much doesn't mean I'm promoting a&nbsp; mentality where we don't chart his music. BTS&nbsp;&nbsp; has only been able to become this mainstream&nbsp; because ARMYs put in the work and supported&nbsp;&nbsp; them with all we had. What I am saying, however,&nbsp; is that regardless of how each member chooses&nbsp;&nbsp; to promote their project, we work with what we&nbsp; have and never demand that they do more. The&nbsp;&nbsp; charts are our concern, not BTS's, and we shouldn't&nbsp; be pressing them to drop more promotional content&nbsp;&nbsp; to satisfy our expectations. It's just not our&nbsp; place to do so. This is why I take issue with&nbsp;&nbsp; people who imply that Big Hit (and when they say&nbsp; Big Hit, they really mean RM) isn't promoting enough&nbsp;&nbsp; for them, and so they're afraid that Indigo won't&nbsp; make the waves like they want it to. Even going as&nbsp;&nbsp; far as to drag Jack In The Box back into this&nbsp; goofy discourse and claim that nobody outside&nbsp;&nbsp; the fandom even knows about it. Basically, what I'm trying to say is that BTS don't have to&nbsp;conform to standard promotional styles just so you can be less insecure about their chart rankings. If anything, what RM proved with the solo projects&nbsp; is that even with unorthodox or minimal promotion,&nbsp;&nbsp; he can still achieve amazing results. Remember&nbsp; mono, a.k.a. the mixtape that ARMYs only had two&nbsp;&nbsp; days of notice for? That debuted at number 26&nbsp; on the Billboard 200 in its first tracking week,&nbsp;&nbsp; despite the fact that it was a mid-week release&nbsp; and had zero physical versions, surpassing Hope World&nbsp;&nbsp; by J-Hope to become the highest charting album by&nbsp;a Korean soloist on the Billboard 200. Not only that,&nbsp;&nbsp; But RM's mixtape became number 1 on iTunes in&nbsp; 86 different countries on its first tracking day,&nbsp;&nbsp; beating out the record of Love Yourself: 轉 Tear--&nbsp; his own group's album--to take the title of the Korean album with the most number 1s on iTunes.&nbsp; As for Indigo, it debuted at number 15 in its first&nbsp;&nbsp; tracking week and then re-peaked at number 3 in&nbsp; its following week, making him the first and only&nbsp;&nbsp; member of BTS at that time to chart that high as&nbsp; a soloist on the Billboard 200. That's not an&nbsp;&nbsp; easy feat. It just goes to show you that there's&nbsp; no need to worry about how BTS's projects are&nbsp;&nbsp; being promoted. Solo stans and diet solo stans&nbsp; even proved this. After Indigo's re-peak at number&nbsp;3,&nbsp; this account tweeted, "We were so scared about&nbsp; the lack of promotion Indigo was receiving, and now,&nbsp; Joon's last archive of his 20s has been achieving&nbsp; so much. Sorry." First of all, that's RM to you.&nbsp;&nbsp; You don't get to call him "Joon" after what you put us&nbsp;through. And second of all, this tweet completely&nbsp;&nbsp; encapsulates everything I hate about the solo&nbsp; stan community. They constantly make problems&nbsp;&nbsp; out of nothing and then work themselves up to&nbsp; the point where they make terrible assumptions&nbsp;&nbsp; about what's going on with their favorite&nbsp; member. Assumptions that they then broadcast&nbsp;&nbsp; to their thousands of followers, making the weeks&nbsp; leading up to releases such a negative experience&nbsp;&nbsp; every single time without fail, only for BTS and&nbsp; ARMYs to continuously prove them wrong. It's so&nbsp;&nbsp; frustrating. One thing I was excited about when BTS&nbsp;announced they would be dropping these solo albums&nbsp;&nbsp; was not just the music, but just the different ways&nbsp; in which the members would promote it. We'd be able&nbsp;&nbsp; to see their individuality every step of the way,&nbsp; and I used to think about how fun it would be to&nbsp;&nbsp; compare and contrast each member's promotional run. But it's clear that because BTS aren't promoting&nbsp;&nbsp; their projects the exact same way, solo stans&nbsp; will never be happy, even though the whole point&nbsp;&nbsp; of Chapter 2 was to showcase BTS's individuality.&nbsp; What happened with Jack In The Box set a dangerous&nbsp;&nbsp; precedent for how each member's solo work should&nbsp;be received. Since its release, we've kept having&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stan issues with each member that has come&nbsp; out with new music, and if nothing is done, we'll&nbsp;&nbsp; likely continue having these problems all the way&nbsp; up until BTS reconvenes as a group in 2025. And I&nbsp;&nbsp; don't know about you, but that does not sound great&nbsp;to me. The second issue I have with solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; is the infantilization of their favorite member.&nbsp; Just so we're all clear on the definition before&nbsp;&nbsp; I continue, to infantilize is to treat a person&nbsp; as a child in a way that denies their maturity&nbsp;&nbsp; in age or experience. Solo stans are notorious&nbsp; for treating their favorite member as a child&nbsp;&nbsp; who cannot speak up for themselves or make any&nbsp;decisions concerning themselves. They believe that&nbsp;&nbsp; their favorite member has no autonomy in their&nbsp; career. There are two things I dislike about this.&nbsp;&nbsp; One, believing your idol has no control over their&nbsp; career further perpetuates the stereotype that&nbsp;&nbsp; all K-pop idols have no freedom in their lives&nbsp; when we know that's not the case. And two, because&nbsp;&nbsp; of this belief that their idol has no control, it&nbsp; allows solo stans to take drastic measures on their&nbsp;&nbsp; favorite members behalf. These drastic measures are&nbsp;always done without the consent of their favorite&nbsp;&nbsp; member and also done without the confirmation&nbsp; that there's even an issue to be raised in the&nbsp;&nbsp; first place. One example I can think of is from&nbsp; March 9th, 2021, when episode 132 of Run BTS, BTS's&nbsp;&nbsp; ongoing variety web show series, was posted on&nbsp; Weverse. The premise behind this episode, and&nbsp;&nbsp; the episode that came before it, was a 77-minute&nbsp; debate where every 11 minutes, the members rotated&nbsp;&nbsp; between being a debater or a moderator and argued&nbsp;over various topics. The catch was that every&nbsp;&nbsp; round, the staff and the current moderator would&nbsp; have already picked out specific words and actions&nbsp;&nbsp; that debaters were forbidden to use during the&nbsp; debate, but the other members didn't know what they&nbsp;&nbsp; were, so every time one of them was used, they got&nbsp; sprayed with water cannons. This was too much for&nbsp;&nbsp; some J-Hope solo stans because that very same day of the episode release, a J-Hope fanbase tweeted&nbsp;&nbsp; this. "In Run BTS episode 132, production staff set&nbsp; unsuitable game levels for the program effect&nbsp;&nbsp; which is a huge pressure physically and mentally&nbsp; for J-Hope. Hope that put the safety of artist&nbsp;&nbsp; in the first place, and unreasonable game can be&nbsp; cancelled." Yes, you heard that correctly. They truly&nbsp;&nbsp; think that J-Hope was put in harm's way...because&nbsp; he was sprayed with water. It's tweets like these&nbsp;&nbsp; that really baffle me because if J-Hope was really&nbsp; uncomfortable with getting sprayed with water,&nbsp;&nbsp; then as an adult with control of his successful&nbsp; career, he would have just not participated.&nbsp; For example, in episode 84 of Run BTS when BTS was doing water activities, J-Hope did not participate&nbsp;&nbsp; in the obstacle course because he was feeling&nbsp; nauseous and took a rest. There was also a time&nbsp;&nbsp; in season 2 episode 6 of Bon Voyage, BTS's travel&nbsp; series, where, after their shark tour on the boat&nbsp;&nbsp; in Hawaii, J-Hope decided to not join BTS to go&nbsp; get food right after because he felt seasick and&nbsp;&nbsp; he was later found resting. In short, these are both&nbsp; examples of J-Hope having the liberty to opt out&nbsp;&nbsp; of activities if he doesn't want to do them. Nobody&nbsp; forces him to keep going, and nobody shames him for&nbsp;&nbsp; not being present either. From this, we can infer&nbsp; two things. One, J-Hope has the ability to decline&nbsp;&nbsp; participating in an activity if he doesn't want&nbsp; to. And two, if he is participating, it's because&nbsp;&nbsp; he wants to and because he's comfortable doing it.&nbsp; So it makes no sense for this J-Hope fanbase to&nbsp;&nbsp; want games like this to get cancelled because he's&nbsp; shown us before that if he's not comfortable with&nbsp;&nbsp; something, he can get up and leave. But the thing&nbsp; that really made this tweet age like expired milk&nbsp;&nbsp; in the hot Arizona sun was the fact that six months&nbsp; later, during Run BTS episode 151, when J Hope was&nbsp;&nbsp; in a hotel room, he was watching that exact same&nbsp; two-parter that the J-Hope account complained&nbsp;&nbsp; about, saying--and I quote--"This is the funniest&nbsp; episode, in my opinion." I don't know about you, but&nbsp;&nbsp; if I was this J-Hope account, I would be incredibly&nbsp; embarrassed if the episodes I campaigned to get&nbsp;&nbsp; cancelled turned out to be my favorite member's (top) episodes out of the entire series. This just goes&nbsp;&nbsp; to show you that not only do solo stans have a&nbsp; problem infantilizing their favorite member, but they&nbsp;&nbsp; also just don't know their favorite member as&nbsp; well as they think they do. And if they were this&nbsp;&nbsp; loud and wrong about one thing, imagine what else&nbsp; they're loud and wrong about. And this isn't the&nbsp;&nbsp; only time J-Hope solo stans have tried to campaign&nbsp;on behalf of J-Hope.Going back to the Jack In The&nbsp;Box&nbsp; incident, the biggest problem solo stans had&nbsp; with the album was that there was no physical CD,&nbsp;&nbsp; amongst other issues they had listed that have&nbsp; their own separate discussions that I just don't&nbsp;&nbsp; have time to get into in this video. But due to&nbsp; the problems they all had with how Jack In The Box&nbsp;&nbsp; was promoted, they decided to plan an event&nbsp; for the 20th and 21st of December, 2022, to trend&nbsp;&nbsp; some demands on Twitter, tagging official accounts,&nbsp;hoping that Big Hit would see their tweets and&nbsp;&nbsp; respond to them. The owner of this account was&nbsp; the main ring leader in all of this and was&nbsp; the one posting information about the event&nbsp; in question and the demands participants were&nbsp;&nbsp; supposed to tweet out once the event had started.&nbsp; Once ARMYs found out about this, the J-Hope&nbsp;&nbsp; account responsible for the post was rightfully&nbsp; jumped, but of course, J-Hope's solo stans weren't budging. For example, this one solo stan says, "Hypocrisy&nbsp; at its finest by OT6 international ARMYs in the&nbsp;&nbsp; quote retweets and replies. Harassing J-Hope fan&nbsp; accounts as always for coming at HYBE's throat&nbsp;&nbsp; and acting blind on their own hypocritical ass." Or&nbsp; this one solo stan who said, "Armpits mad about a&nbsp;&nbsp; hashtag directed to the company like they will get&nbsp; directly affected by it. HYBE are grown people, they&nbsp;&nbsp; can address or deal with the issues themselves.&nbsp; They don't need Ratmys to speak for them. You&nbsp;&nbsp; company stans are weird." Firstly, I have already&nbsp; established in the first part of this video that&nbsp;&nbsp; J-Hope made the creative decision to make the&nbsp; album CD-less to fit the vibe of his concept. This was not an executive decision by higher-ups at HYBE who exercised their authority over J-Hope to&nbsp;&nbsp; force him into taking the CD out of his album. So&nbsp; that means that this isn't a hashtag directed at&nbsp;&nbsp; the company, this is ultimately a hashtag directed&nbsp; towards J-Hope himself. Because whether these solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stans want to admit it or not, J-Hope said out&nbsp; of his own mouth that he made the decision for&nbsp;&nbsp; this album to be CD-less. Also, going back to what&nbsp; the second solo stan tweeted, they said that&nbsp;&nbsp; HYBE are grown people and can therefore handle these&nbsp;issues themselves and don't need us to speak up&nbsp;&nbsp; for them. It is absolutely crazy how they can tweet&nbsp; something like this without realizing that we can&nbsp;&nbsp; use that exact same logic against them as to why&nbsp; they don't need to campaign for J-Hope because&nbsp;&nbsp; J-Hope is also a grown man that has been training&nbsp; and working within the K-pop industry since 2010,&nbsp;&nbsp; and if that's the case, why does J-Hope need solo&nbsp; stans to speak up for him? And this isn't even the&nbsp;&nbsp; craziest instance I've seen of solo stans trying&nbsp; to take action on behalf of their favorite member.&nbsp;&nbsp; On September 6th, 2022, this account dedicated&nbsp; to BTS member V posted a thread detailing an&nbsp;&nbsp; official letter they sent to HYBE through a law&nbsp; firm. Within that letter, they listed out demands&nbsp;&nbsp; that they wanted HYBE to carry out to protect&nbsp; V from those that wanted to cause harm to him,&nbsp;&nbsp; even going on to suggest that fans have the right&nbsp; to claim compensation for the mental and material&nbsp;&nbsp; pain caused to both the artist and the fan. At the&nbsp; bottom of the thread, they also include a link to&nbsp;&nbsp; donate to the account's PayPal if anyone wants to&nbsp; help out. This thread garnered a lot of attention&nbsp;&nbsp; until it reached the timeline of this account&nbsp; known as the BTS ARMY Bar Association, which is&nbsp;&nbsp; an account run by actual lawyers that are fans of&nbsp; BTS, and what they had to say about this V-focused&nbsp;&nbsp; account was not good. I'll link the full thread&nbsp; in the description if you want to read it, but to&nbsp;&nbsp; summarize what they said about the whole situation, one, you cannot file a lawsuit on behalf of someone&nbsp;&nbsp; else without that person's express consent; two,&nbsp; you cannot receive compensation for actions taken&nbsp;&nbsp; against someone else; three, ordering an individual&nbsp; or their representative to take legal action in&nbsp;&nbsp; this way is unethical and, if there are any threats&nbsp; of any kind, can be considered blackmail; four, fans&nbsp;&nbsp; do not have rights to any information regarding&nbsp; legal action taken by V or his representatives;&nbsp;&nbsp; and five, Big Hit already warned fans that it's&nbsp; difficult for them to assist or join third-party&nbsp;&nbsp; complaints. Let me expand a bit on the fifth&nbsp; point. On December 26th, 2019, in a Weverse notice&nbsp;&nbsp; updating fans on legal proceeding against the&nbsp; violation of artist rights, they specify near the&nbsp;&nbsp; end of the notice, "Big Hit's legal team is overseeing&nbsp;the legal proceedings against these malicious&nbsp;&nbsp; postings. For third-party complaints brought forth&nbsp; by fans or other individuals, please note that&nbsp;&nbsp; there is considerable difficulty in proceeding&nbsp; with such cases, since third-party complaints&nbsp;&nbsp; require the artist to be summoned by the police&nbsp; as defendants to appear in person for questioning.&nbsp;&nbsp; Such appearances would be difficult to arrange&nbsp; due to the artist's schedules as well as other&nbsp;&nbsp; limiting factors. Thus, we must inform you that it&nbsp; is difficult for the company to provide assistance&nbsp;&nbsp; with or join such third-party complaints submitted&nbsp; by individuals or organizations." What Big Hit was&nbsp;&nbsp; not-so-subtly trying to tell us is that we should&nbsp; refrain from bringing forth third-party complaints&nbsp;&nbsp; because they are a great inconvenience to the&nbsp; artist. When you file a third-party complaint, the&nbsp;&nbsp; artist would need to be summoned by police and&nbsp; stay for questioning. And with how busy BTS can&nbsp;&nbsp; sometimes be every waking second of the day, asking&nbsp;them to interrupt their already packed schedules&nbsp;&nbsp; to sit down in a police station for God knows&nbsp; how long is just not feasible. And even if they&nbsp;&nbsp; weren't busy, why should they have to constantly&nbsp; drop everything they're doing to be questioned&nbsp;&nbsp; by police every time a fan files a complaint? Big&nbsp; Hit already goes out of their way to file legal&nbsp;&nbsp; complaints as BTS's representatives and gives us&nbsp; periodic updates whenever they've made progress.&nbsp;&nbsp; They even gave us an official email to send things&nbsp; if we have any cases of malicious comments that we&nbsp;&nbsp; need to report. Is this not a better alternative&nbsp; that not only guarantees that Big Hit is able to&nbsp;&nbsp; have a collection of evidence in one place, but as&nbsp; BTS's representatives, they don't need to call BTS&nbsp;&nbsp; out of their houses every time they need to file&nbsp; a complaint to the police. On top of that, this&nbsp;&nbsp; V-focused account is taking donations to cover fees, but as BTS ARMY Bar Association pointed out, you&nbsp;&nbsp; shouldn't give people money for a lawsuit unless&nbsp; you can verify that there's an active lawsuit with&nbsp;&nbsp; the victim's permission and involvement, there is&nbsp; some form of the proof of the cost of the lawsuit&nbsp;&nbsp; such as a legal invoice, and that there is evidence&nbsp; of the victim's financial status, such that you are&nbsp;&nbsp; satisfied that they actually need help paying&nbsp; their legal bills, and this V-focused account&nbsp;&nbsp; has provided none of that. So you can imagine how&nbsp; many people must have donated to this person's&nbsp;&nbsp; PayPal without receiving verification that the&nbsp; money they're sending is even going towards what&nbsp;&nbsp; it's supposed to go toward. All in all, you can see&nbsp; that solo stans will take all sorts of measures&nbsp;&nbsp; to supposedly protect their favorite member, and&nbsp; I haven't even touched on other extreme cases&nbsp;&nbsp; such as solo stans sending trucks with TV screens&nbsp; to HYBE's building that broadcasts their demands&nbsp; for everyone to see. That's something you can&nbsp; research on your own. But all of this is just&nbsp;&nbsp; to show how far solo stans take things, even if it&nbsp; means literally embarrassing their favorite member&nbsp;&nbsp; at their place of work. The third issue I have&nbsp; with solo stans is the support that they give&nbsp;&nbsp; to sasaengs and fansites. Let's start by defining&nbsp; these terms first. The word sasaeng derives from&nbsp;&nbsp; the Korean word 'sasaeng hwal', meaning private life. Sasaengs are known as over-obsessive fans that&nbsp;&nbsp; go out of their way to stalk their favorite&nbsp; celebrities for attention and sometimes even&nbsp;&nbsp; go out of their way to harm them. As for fansites, fansites are fans who take photos and&nbsp;&nbsp; videos of idols using professional-grade cameras.&nbsp; My issue with sasaengs is fairly obvious. Stalking&nbsp;&nbsp; is clearly a violation of both the official Big&nbsp; Hit fan etiquette and the actual law. Going out&nbsp;&nbsp; of your way to invade anybody's privacy and put&nbsp; them in harm's way is unethical, no matter how&nbsp;&nbsp; you look at it. Stalking celebrities is something&nbsp; that, unfortunately, is pretty common in all parts&nbsp;&nbsp; of the world, but in South Korea, I just feel like&nbsp; it's more intense? There might be a couple reasons&nbsp;&nbsp; for that. First off, the sasaeng community is pretty&nbsp; organized. It's said that sasaengs are able to obtain&nbsp;&nbsp; private information of celebrities by working&nbsp; in places like phone companies and airports,&nbsp;&nbsp; which they can either use for themselves to stalk&nbsp; and call their favorite celebrities, or they can&nbsp;&nbsp; sell that information to others for more money. And&nbsp; it's these jobs, along with the selling of private&nbsp;&nbsp; information, that funds all the trips they take to&nbsp; stalk their favorite celebrities around the world.&nbsp;&nbsp; As to why they stalk their favorite celebrities,&nbsp; at the end of the day, it's all to get noticed.&nbsp;&nbsp; The bottom line is that they want attention from&nbsp; the celebrities they stalk, and so will try to get&nbsp;&nbsp; close to them through any means necessary even if&nbsp;it means endangering the safety of others. This is&nbsp;&nbsp; what makes them especially dangerous, because they&nbsp;don't actually care whether the attention they get&nbsp;&nbsp; from their favorite celebrities is positive or&nbsp; negative. The fact that they've been given any&nbsp;&nbsp; attention at all is what eggs them on. And this&nbsp; is not me putting any blame on celebrities who&nbsp;&nbsp; do react, because they're well within their right to&nbsp; do so. It's it's just a shame that stalking hadn't&nbsp;&nbsp; been taken as seriously in South Korea for the&nbsp; longest time, despite the horror stories that some&nbsp;&nbsp; idols have shared about their own experience with&nbsp; sasaengs. This also brings me to my second reason as&nbsp;&nbsp; to why the sasaeng community is so intense in&nbsp; South Korea. Up until 2021, stalking was barely&nbsp;&nbsp; considered an actual crime in South Korea. It was&nbsp; previously classified in the criminal code as misdemeanor, which would only land you a 100,000&nbsp;won fine at most, which is less than the cost of&nbsp;&nbsp; a speeding ticket in Washington DC. But now, effective from October (21st), 2021, the new anti-stalking law&nbsp;&nbsp; makes it so that perpetrators can face up to three&nbsp; years in prison. This is the first time such a law&nbsp;&nbsp; has ever been put into place, so imagine all the&nbsp; K-pop idols and other celebrities in South Korea&nbsp;&nbsp; that have had to deal with sasaengs for decades without&nbsp;the perpetrators having any way to be punished&nbsp;&nbsp; outside a slap on the wrist. Like I said, stalking&nbsp; is against the official Big Hit fan etiquette,&nbsp;&nbsp; but because of how lax the laws were around&nbsp; stalking, it could only be taken as more of a firm&nbsp;&nbsp; suggestion because the sasaengs who were caught never&nbsp;really faced proper consequences and were more&nbsp;&nbsp; than willing to pay a measly fine however many&nbsp; times they needed to, as long as they could keep&nbsp;&nbsp; stalking their favorite idol. But now that this&nbsp; law has been put into place, I guarantee the next&nbsp;&nbsp; updates Big Hit gives us on legal proceedings will&nbsp; include updates relating to stalking as well. An example I can give in regard to stalking&nbsp; has to do with Jin's enlistment. As some of you may&nbsp;&nbsp; know, Jin officially enlisted in the South Korean&nbsp; military on the 13th of December, 2022, to fulfill&nbsp;&nbsp; his mandatory service of 18 months. A few weeks&nbsp; before his enlistment, however, the location of&nbsp;&nbsp; the military site he would be training at was&nbsp; leaked to the public and spread around on social&nbsp;&nbsp; media. It was so widespread that eventually Jin&nbsp; himself found out and took to Weverse on the 23rd&nbsp;&nbsp; of November, 2022, to say this. "There are articles&nbsp; released that I didn't want, but please don't come&nbsp;&nbsp; to the training center, ARMY. There will be many&nbsp; people besides me, so it can be dangerous because&nbsp;&nbsp; it's crowded. ARMY, I love you." This was followed up by Big Hit making an official Weverse statement on&nbsp;&nbsp; December 5th, 2022, telling people not to visit&nbsp; the military site for his entrance ceremony. As&nbsp;&nbsp; a result, Jin's entrance ceremony was an event&nbsp; that was only attended by his family members&nbsp;&nbsp; and the other members of BTS. I will come back&nbsp; to this point in a moment, so please hold onto&nbsp;it.&nbsp; After Jin officially enlisted, suddenly there&nbsp; was a rise in accounts giving updates on Jin in&nbsp;&nbsp; the military. People were naturally weirded out&nbsp; because nobody was sure how exactly people were&nbsp;&nbsp; figuring out what Jin was doing on a daily basis&nbsp; and spreading pictures that were not posted by&nbsp;&nbsp; any of BTS's or Big Hit's official accounts, so&nbsp; a lot of questions were asked. Something that&nbsp;&nbsp; continuously came up when asking about where&nbsp; certain information came from was this website&nbsp;&nbsp; called The Camp, which is apparently dedicated&nbsp; to giving updates about those enlisted, mainly&nbsp;&nbsp; for family, close friends, etc. So because this&nbsp; website was public, Jin solo stans were saying&nbsp;&nbsp; it's okay to use since, according to them, it was&nbsp; public information and, therefore, there should be&nbsp;&nbsp; no problem posting updates from that site. But&nbsp; there's a catch. Remember how I said that the&nbsp;&nbsp; location of Jin's base was leaked, and that Jin&nbsp; had made a post on Weverse talking about how he&nbsp;&nbsp; never wanted that information released? Well, one&nbsp; ARMY decided to take one for the team and try to&nbsp;&nbsp; make an account on The Camp website to see how&nbsp; easy it is to get updates on specific people. And,&nbsp;&nbsp; lo and behold, in order to get updates about Jin,&nbsp; you have to know where he's enlisted. That means&nbsp;&nbsp; despite Jin voicing his upset at the leaked&nbsp; information, solo stans still went behind his back&nbsp;&nbsp; and used that information to access his updates&nbsp; on The Camp website. And that's not all. Not only&nbsp;&nbsp; were solo stans using the details of his base's&nbsp; address to access the website, but they were also&nbsp;&nbsp; using it to send letters to his base, their logic&nbsp; being that Jin can't update us himself since he&nbsp;&nbsp; allegedly was not allowed to use a cell phone,&nbsp; which meant social media updates from him were&nbsp;&nbsp; completely off the table. It also meant that the&nbsp; only way for Jin to see support from fans&nbsp;&nbsp; is if they send physical letters. But then on&nbsp; January 4th, 2023, Big Hit posted another Weverse&nbsp;&nbsp; statement asking fans not to send any mail at all&nbsp; for the duration of his enlistment, as it would be&nbsp;&nbsp; difficult to store things such as letters and&nbsp; gifts, and that they could easily be lost. They&nbsp;&nbsp; also asked fans not to show up for his training&nbsp; graduation ceremony either. An alternative they&nbsp;&nbsp; did provide, however, was for ARMYs to send Jin&nbsp; supportive messages on Weverse using the hashtag&nbsp;&nbsp; 'Dear Jin from ARMY', stating, "Big Hit music will assist&nbsp;in making sure Jin is able to personally see any&nbsp;&nbsp; kind and warm messages fans leave on Weverse." This means that Jin will be given opportunities while&nbsp;&nbsp; enlisted to read Weverse messages from ARMYs. On&nbsp;top of that, since Jin started his service, multiple&nbsp;&nbsp; BTS members on multiple different occasions would&nbsp;tell us whenever Jin had talked to them and update&nbsp;&nbsp; us on how he is. If that wasn't enough, since his&nbsp; initial training period finished, Jin himself comes&nbsp;&nbsp; online every so often to post pictures, reply&nbsp; to posts from other BTS members, or comment&nbsp;&nbsp; on their live streams. And if even that wasn't&nbsp; enough, he went out to dinner with the members&nbsp;&nbsp; when he was on a short leave once, he took time&nbsp; off to attend J-Hope's military entrance ceremony,&nbsp;&nbsp; and he and J-Hope even attended Suga's final concert&nbsp;on his D-Day tour in Seoul. This goes against every&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stan and diet solo stan account claiming&nbsp; that Jin wouldn't have access to a cell phone. If&nbsp;&nbsp; he's able to call the BTS members, text them either&nbsp; individually or in their group chat, and even post&nbsp;&nbsp; pictures to Weverse and Instagram, then obviously he is not as restricted as solo stans said he was, and&nbsp;&nbsp; if that's the case, they have absolutely no way to&nbsp; justify using websites such as The Camp for Jin&nbsp;&nbsp; updates since we're able to get updates from the&nbsp; other BTS members and also Jin himself. There are&nbsp;&nbsp; multiple takeaways from this whole fiasco, in my&nbsp; opinion. The first is that not all information that&nbsp;&nbsp; is official and confirmed is good, because if the&nbsp; media wasn't afraid to leak his base's location,&nbsp;&nbsp; how can we be sure that anything not coming from&nbsp; BTS or Big Hit has been released with Jin's consent? The second is that good things come to those who&nbsp; wait. I never participated in digging around the&nbsp;&nbsp; web for things like what Jin's daily schedule&nbsp; is or what he's eating every day. I just didn't&nbsp;&nbsp; trust that any of this information was released&nbsp; consensually, and so I chose to wait for updates&nbsp;&nbsp; from the members instead, so when BTS did actually&nbsp;update us on how he's doing, and then we got those&nbsp;&nbsp; pictures and other posts from Jin, it felt so much&nbsp; more rewarding and I felt so much better knowing&nbsp;&nbsp; that these updates were consensual, and that&nbsp; I didn't have to use underhanded, convoluted&nbsp;&nbsp; methods to get them. And the third is that there&nbsp; are some people who truly have unhealthy coping&nbsp;&nbsp; mechanisms. I miss Jin just as much as the next&nbsp; ARMY and I'm practically counting down the days&nbsp;&nbsp; until he comes back, but there are plenty of ways&nbsp; I can channel that energy into something more&nbsp;&nbsp; productive. Instead of resorting to borderline&nbsp; sasaeng methods of getting crumbs of information&nbsp;&nbsp; about Jin from a government website, I could stream&nbsp;Jin's music, I could watch his (web)show appearances, or&nbsp;&nbsp; I could send him a message on Weverse that I know&nbsp;he would actually be able to see. We didn't know&nbsp;&nbsp; what Jin was doing every single day before he&nbsp; enlisted, so I don't see a reason to now. And if&nbsp;&nbsp; that's information that you need to know in order&nbsp; to feel emotionally stable, then that's not healthy,&nbsp;&nbsp; and I would suggest you wean yourself off being&nbsp; so dependent on such updates that you'd willingly&nbsp;&nbsp; override Jin's consent to get them. As for fansites, my problems with them are similar but also&nbsp;&nbsp; not entirely the same. While I wouldn't say that&nbsp; every fansite is a sasaeng, there are plenty of&nbsp;&nbsp; sasaengs that are also fansites, taking pictures&nbsp; and videos of BTS in places that are either not&nbsp;&nbsp; permitted or were never disclosed to the public.&nbsp; One of these incidents happened in late 2022, back&nbsp;&nbsp; when V went on an unannounced trip to Paris. Nobody&nbsp;knew about where he went or for how long, and yet&nbsp;&nbsp; videos circulated online of V being recorded in&nbsp; the airports. One fansite account that had gotten&nbsp;&nbsp; some pictures of him during this time and was&nbsp; rightfully called out for it, ended up making a&nbsp;&nbsp; statement under their post saying, For those who&nbsp; suspected me of being a sasaeng or stalker, I'd&nbsp;&nbsp; like to clarify that I asked a friend in&nbsp;Paris to assist me in photographing this French&nbsp;&nbsp; boarding after I knew Taehyung's arrival time in&nbsp; Korea. My fansite is run by two people, so we&nbsp;&nbsp; can shoot trips in Korea and overseas. If there are&nbsp; similar doubts in the future, I will not explain&nbsp;&nbsp; again and I will not let my friend take pictures&nbsp; anymore about Taehyung's future trip in France."&nbsp;&nbsp; Keep in mind that I just said that V's trip to Paris&nbsp; was not announced to the public, so there's no&nbsp;&nbsp; way that anyone should have known about it, which&nbsp; begs the question: if no one was supposed to know,&nbsp;&nbsp; about it then how did this fansite know V's&nbsp; arrival time in Korea? They must have gotten this&nbsp;&nbsp; information from somewhere right? Well, the answer&nbsp;is obvious. They either bought the information off&nbsp;&nbsp; a sasaeng, or they are the sasaeng who acquired&nbsp; these flight details through illegal unethical&nbsp;&nbsp; means. There's just no dancing around this fact. And&nbsp;yet, this post with these airport pictures got so&nbsp;&nbsp; much engagement even, though this goes directly&nbsp; against all the rules. And some common arguments&nbsp;&nbsp; I've seen for why fansites like this shouldn't&nbsp; be called out for stalking V are things things&nbsp;&nbsp; like: "he was smiling" or "he waved at us". And because&nbsp;of that, it should somehow make stalking him okay.&nbsp;&nbsp; But at the end of the day, it doesn't. It's still&nbsp; against the official Big Hit fan etiquette and&nbsp;&nbsp; it's still against the law. No amount of smiling&nbsp; and waving on V's part will change that. And oftentimes&nbsp;&nbsp; when they're at the airport, BTS have to&nbsp; maintain politeness in front of reporters and&nbsp;&nbsp; journalists, otherwise they risk getting bad&nbsp; publicity. Just look at how V was reported on&nbsp;&nbsp; the one time he decided to not greet any of the&nbsp; cameras on his way out of the airport after coming&nbsp;&nbsp; back from Paris Fashion Week in late 2022. He's&nbsp; even taken more direct approaches in the past,&nbsp;&nbsp; like in 2018 when fansites and sasaengs wouldn't&nbsp; let him walk through the airport in peace, so he&nbsp;&nbsp; turned to the nearest one and shoved his phone&nbsp; in their face to give them a taste of their own&nbsp;&nbsp; medicine. And he ended up getting bashed for this,&nbsp; too. This leads to them having to be more polite&nbsp;&nbsp; when telling people off, otherwise they are painted&nbsp; by the media as the bad guy. An example of this is&nbsp;&nbsp; a Weverse livestream Jungkook did on March 3rd,&nbsp; 2023, where he said, "I'm really thankful that you&nbsp;&nbsp; guys pay attention to me but... when I'm working out, please don't come looking for me. I got shocked the&nbsp;&nbsp; last time. Other ARMYs could (look for me), but they&nbsp;don't do it, you know? There's this line of privacy&nbsp;&nbsp; (for each other). I'm just a person and ARMYs&nbsp; are just people too, but let's not do stuff like this."&nbsp;&nbsp; So, as you can see, their images constantly in&nbsp; danger of getting dragged through the mud whenever&nbsp;&nbsp; they decide to not play into what journalists and&nbsp; so-called fans want want them to do. And that's&nbsp;&nbsp; not fair to BTS or any celebrity in general. And&nbsp; my critiques of fansites don't end there either.&nbsp;&nbsp; Some people could watch this section of the video&nbsp; and still claim that fansites are still largely unproblematic because most of them don't look up private information. But that doesn't mean that they don't break the rules. Fan etiquette regarding airport rules aside, there are plenty of fansites that go out of their way to ignore Big Hit's rules regarding camera policies. For the longest time, whenever BTS go on tour, the general rule is that you are not allowed to bring professional&nbsp; cameras into the venue. The only thing you're allowed to bring aside from your phone are&nbsp;nonprofessional still cameras for personal use and the point-and-shoot cameras without&nbsp; detachable lenses. But even with these rules&nbsp;&nbsp; so clearly laid out, fansites will still find a&nbsp; way to sneak their camera equipment in and take&nbsp;&nbsp; photos. On April 17th, 2022, during BTS's PTD concert&nbsp;run in Vegas, one account tweeted, "Person standing&nbsp;&nbsp; right next to me was just kicked out of the BTS&nbsp; concert for taking photos with the professional&nbsp;&nbsp; camera. Like 5 security guards came up and they&nbsp; did not mess around. Pretty wild." Not only does this&nbsp;&nbsp; show that fansites were breaking the rules, but&nbsp; it shows that Big Hit takes the camera policy very&nbsp;&nbsp; seriously. There is no reason why fansites should&nbsp; be smuggling camera equipment into the concert. It poses a possible threat to concert-goers because&nbsp;the camera equipment could be mistaken for a&nbsp;&nbsp; weapon and cause panic in the crowd, and also, we&nbsp; already get so many HD photos of BTS concerts&nbsp;&nbsp; these days from Big Hit themselves that&nbsp; fansites have become pretty obsolete, especially&nbsp;&nbsp; considering that, up until BTS's first PTD concert&nbsp; run in Los Angeles back in November of 2021, we had&nbsp;&nbsp; gone two years without relying on fansite photos&nbsp; from concerts during the pandemic, and the fandom&nbsp;&nbsp; was just fine without them. We've already proved&nbsp; that ARMYs don't need them to survive, so anyone&nbsp;&nbsp; trying to convince you that fansites are an&nbsp; absolute necessity are completely delusional.&nbsp;&nbsp; Besides, why give support to fansites, who already&nbsp; have a bad reputation of being rude during&nbsp;&nbsp; concerts while making profit of taking BTS's&nbsp; pictures on top of that, when you can get the same&nbsp;&nbsp; high-quality pictures from BTS's Weverse community that have been ethically taken for no extra charge&nbsp;&nbsp; at all. Just some food for thought. From all the&nbsp; screenshots I've shown you of solo stans, you'll&nbsp;&nbsp; start to notice a pattern with all of them. They&nbsp; all hate Big Hit Music and its parent company, HYBE,&nbsp;&nbsp; and constantly express their animosity towards the&nbsp; Big Hit founder and current chairman of HYBE Bang Sihyuk,&nbsp;&nbsp; a.k.a. Bang PD. They also expressed their animosity&nbsp;towards the staff who work for him and also BTS's&nbsp;&nbsp; decision to sign to the Big Hit label. The reason&nbsp; why is simple. For solo stans, Big Hit and HYBE&nbsp;&nbsp; are the source of all their problems. They're the&nbsp; companies who supposedly mistreat their favorite&nbsp;&nbsp; member and never give them the opportunity to&nbsp; shine, regardless of how transparent Big Hit&nbsp;and HYBE is with how they operate and how they treat&nbsp; the members, regardless of the praise they get&nbsp;&nbsp; from insiders in the industry, Big Hit and HYBE&nbsp; are irredeemable in solo stans' eyes. Nothing&nbsp;&nbsp; they ever do is worth merit unless it's to the&nbsp; benefit of their favorite member, and even then,&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stans treat it as the bare minimum and move on. Anyone in the fandom who praises or defends Big&nbsp;Hit and HYBE is up for scrutiny by solo stans&nbsp; as well. It's not uncommon for solo stans to&nbsp;&nbsp; call ARMYs "company stans", claiming that we support&nbsp;the vision of the company more than we support the&nbsp;&nbsp; actual members, and that we would not hesitate to&nbsp; throw BTS under the bus in the defense of Big Hit&nbsp;&nbsp; and HYBE. Even though I normally don't have a problem&nbsp;with how BTS's label runs things, I don't believe&nbsp;&nbsp; that Big Hit or HYBE are above criticism, something&nbsp; that most--if not all--ARMYs would agree with, I've&nbsp;&nbsp; had no problems with publicly talking about&nbsp; anything Big Hit or HYBE has done that I didn't agree with. But since they don't evidently make such&nbsp; egregious mistakes with their artists' management&nbsp;&nbsp; like other music companies do, I never really have&nbsp; much to say in regards to how they can do better.&nbsp;&nbsp; And it's because of this that solo stans have a&nbsp; problem with ARMYs since they believe their&nbsp;&nbsp; artist is always being mistreated. For armies&nbsp; to have no serious issues with the company is&nbsp;&nbsp; infuriating to them. The message they take away&nbsp; from our lack of outrage is that we don't care&nbsp;&nbsp; about their favorite member being mistreated.&nbsp; But again, the mistreatment they fight against&nbsp;&nbsp; are things that BTS have never complained about.&nbsp; The reason why most ARMYs don't spend their days&nbsp;&nbsp; harassing Big Hit and HYBE over unfounded claims&nbsp; is because of how much BTS has shown that they&nbsp;&nbsp; have control over their career. For example, when&nbsp; HYBE (which was formerly known as Big Hit until&nbsp;&nbsp; they separated the year after) went public in&nbsp; October of 2020, Bang PD gifted 683,385 HYBE&nbsp;&nbsp; common shares to each member of BTS worth over $7&nbsp;million. By making the member shareholders of HYBE, that means they actually own a portion of their&nbsp; company right now and, according to an article on&nbsp;&nbsp; Investopedia titled 'Know Your Shareholder Rights',&nbsp; being a common shareholder gives you voting power&nbsp;&nbsp; on major issues, right to transfer ownership of&nbsp; your stock, entitlement to dividends, opportunities&nbsp;&nbsp; to inspect corporate books and records, and even&nbsp; the right to sue for wrongful acts. The last right&nbsp;&nbsp; is a especially important within the context of&nbsp; this video because by Bang PD gifting these shares&nbsp;&nbsp; to BTS, he also gave them the right to file a&nbsp; shareholder lawsuit against their label, should BTS&nbsp;&nbsp; feel wronged in any way. So the fact that BTS has&nbsp; this right and has not used it should give you a&nbsp;&nbsp; clue as to just how much they are being mistreated. This is why BTS felt comfortable enough to renew&nbsp;&nbsp; their contract for an extra seven years. They trusted that Big Hit would continue to support them the&nbsp;&nbsp; way they wanted, and that their new contracts&nbsp; had everything they were looking for. and this issue also comes with the problem of&nbsp; people seeing Big Hit and HYBE as a monolith and&nbsp;&nbsp; not as companies with hundreds of employees at&nbsp; every level. It's not just Bang PD pulling all&nbsp;&nbsp; the strings behind the scenes. Big Hit and HYBE's&nbsp; teams are also full of makeup artists, stylists&nbsp;&nbsp; choreographers, in-house producers, creative&nbsp; directors, data analysts, managers, and more; some of&nbsp;&nbsp; which have been with BTS from the very beginning,&nbsp; which means they also had their own come-up story&nbsp; alongside them. BTS have always had such great&nbsp; things to say about their staff, even going as&nbsp;&nbsp; far as to dedicate songs to them like For Youth.&nbsp; They also have a great relationship with Bang PD&nbsp;&nbsp; that has become so close that they even refer to&nbsp; him with the 'hyung' honorific, which is a huge deal&nbsp;&nbsp; given South Korea's intricate respect culture.&nbsp; So it always baffles me whenever solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; try to make it seem like every employee on every&nbsp; level hates their favorite member in particular&nbsp;&nbsp; and wants to see them fail, when pretty much every&nbsp; interaction BTS has with their staff and with Bang PD&nbsp;&nbsp; is always so wholesome. The reason why ARMYs&nbsp; trust BTS's label is because BTS trusts their&nbsp;&nbsp; label, and we trust BTS. If BTS ever vocalizes&nbsp; that they ever have a serious issue with how&nbsp;&nbsp; they're being managed, then best believe all bets&nbsp; are off. So if BTS are satisfied with their label,&nbsp;&nbsp; then who are we to protest in their place? It's&nbsp; bad enough that solo stans treat their favorite&nbsp;&nbsp; members like children and treat Big Hit and HYBE as the devil reincarnate, but they also do not&nbsp;&nbsp; like ARMYs either. The reason why they don't like&nbsp; ARMYs is because ARMYs put their trust in the&nbsp;&nbsp; boys and their label to make decisions regarding&nbsp; the group's management and promotion, and as we've&nbsp;&nbsp; discussed Big Hit and HYBE are solo stans'&nbsp; mortal enemy, so naturally they believe we've&nbsp;&nbsp; been brainwashed into accepting the mistreatment&nbsp; of BTS while solo stans are apparently the only&nbsp;&nbsp; ones who know the truth. They're not mindless&nbsp; sheep like us ARMYs are. They've ascended past&nbsp;&nbsp; our ways of primitive thinking. (That was... that was&nbsp;sarcasm, by the way.) Because solo stans don't&nbsp;&nbsp; trust any community but their own, they're very&nbsp; skeptical of ARMYs and have this idea that none&nbsp;&nbsp; of us truly love all the members, but especially&nbsp; their favorite member. Every solo stan for every&nbsp;&nbsp; member of BTS has the exact same belief that&nbsp; ARMYs do not appreciate their favorite member&nbsp;&nbsp; enough. If you take the sentence "ARMYs don't&nbsp; appreciate _____ enough", solo stans will put&nbsp;&nbsp; any member's name in there and then spread that&nbsp; lie to other solo stans to increase the tension&nbsp;&nbsp; between our communities. It's gotten to a point&nbsp; where any ARMY that disagrees with a solo stan&nbsp;&nbsp; is deemed an anti of these solo stans' favorite&nbsp; member. It's a common tactic used by solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; to manipulate ARMYs into believing that they&nbsp; don't actually love BTS. Solo stans have this&nbsp;&nbsp; 'holier than thou' mentality in their eyes; their&nbsp; community has the most morals and could never do&nbsp;&nbsp; anything wrong. Every action they take is towards a&nbsp; righteous cause, which is wanting better treatment&nbsp;&nbsp; for their favorite member, according to them. So if&nbsp; an ARMY disagrees with them, it means that ARMY's&nbsp;&nbsp; beliefs are not as righteous as their beliefs,&nbsp; and by extension, don't care about a specific&nbsp;&nbsp; member, making that ARMY OT6. And this isn't to say&nbsp;that ARMYs are always perfect themselves.&nbsp;&nbsp; There are plenty of incidents where an ARMY might say&nbsp;something ignorant or disrespectful, and we as a&nbsp;&nbsp; fandom have to go in and educate them. Even I have&nbsp;been in such positions where I've accidentally&nbsp;&nbsp; crossed the line and needed to be corrected. But&nbsp; my issue with solo stans is that they'll put a&nbsp;&nbsp; target on these ARMYs' backs and continue to harass&nbsp;them even after that ARMY has been educated and&nbsp;&nbsp; changed for the better. It doesn't matter if you&nbsp; genuinely apologized, and it doesn't matter if&nbsp;&nbsp; you've never made that same mistake again. If you&nbsp; cross solo stans once, you strikeout for life.&nbsp;&nbsp; And when it comes to the threshold that solo stans&nbsp; have to judge whether or not someone is an anti&nbsp;&nbsp; of their favorite member, it is very low. I've&nbsp; seen ARMYs get attacked for things that you&nbsp;&nbsp; wouldn't even believe if I showed you, like the&nbsp; time an ARMY decided to make a thread comparing&nbsp;&nbsp; the color scheme of Jungkook's hair and outfits to&nbsp; the color scheme of Nike sneakers, and they got&nbsp;&nbsp; spammed with so much hate and accusations they&nbsp; had to tweet an apology and clarification. Or like&nbsp;&nbsp; the time job solo stans tried to cancel an ARMY&nbsp; because they couldn't be bothered to type out the&nbsp;&nbsp; hyphen in J-Hope's stage name, even though there are posts from BTS's official Twitter where J-Hope&nbsp;&nbsp; himself doesn't type out the hyphen in his stage&nbsp; name. A while back, I was called a Jin anti simply&nbsp;&nbsp; because I said that if Jin ever wanted to act, it's&nbsp; ultimately his choice and we should just listen to&nbsp;&nbsp; him because it's his life. So you can see the type&nbsp; of petty reasons solo stans will use to try and&nbsp;&nbsp; cancel just about anybody on Twitter. If they can't&nbsp; find damning enough evidence, then they'll take&nbsp;&nbsp; any regular tweet of yours, twist it to fit their&nbsp; narrative, and send their solo stan friends after&nbsp;&nbsp; you with the ultimate goal to get you to either&nbsp; grovel and beg for their forgiveness, or deactivate.&nbsp;&nbsp; Now to move on to my final issue I have with&nbsp; solo stans, which is the way that they constantly&nbsp;&nbsp; ignore everything BTS and Big Hit say in order for&nbsp; their narratives to be effective. There are many&nbsp;&nbsp; instances I can think of where solo stans blame&nbsp; the company or other people for decisions revealed&nbsp;&nbsp; to be made by the members. They would sooner ignore&nbsp;the words of their own favorite member than admit&nbsp;&nbsp; they aren't the puppet solo stans make them out&nbsp; to be. As a result, they believe a plethora of false&nbsp;&nbsp; information that can very easily be debunked if&nbsp; you just listen to what BTS and their label have&nbsp;&nbsp; said out of their own mouths. You could literally&nbsp; show the evidence to solo stans, and they'll still&nbsp;&nbsp; tell you that they don't have to believe or follow&nbsp; what their favorite member says. They're just too&nbsp;&nbsp; far gone to listen to reason anymore. I hate to&nbsp; beat a dead horse, but to refer back to the Jack In The Box&nbsp;&nbsp; incident, a documentary on the album-making&nbsp; process as well as the Lollapalooza performance of it&nbsp;&nbsp; was relased released on Disney+ and Weverse on&nbsp; the 17th of February, 2023, called J-Hope In The Box.&nbsp;&nbsp; In the ending credits of the documentary, J-Hope&nbsp; said in an interview, "When my album had just been&nbsp;&nbsp; released, many things worried me. 'Shouldn't there be&nbsp;more promotion efforts?' 'Why aren't you dancing?' that&nbsp;&nbsp; kind of feedback did bother me, so I was quite&nbsp; anxious. But now that I look back, the fans seem&nbsp;&nbsp; to love it, and the project seems to have been a&nbsp; success." ARMYs immediately pointed out what he said&nbsp;&nbsp; and referred back to all the times J-Hope solo&nbsp; stans complained about lack of promotion. They&nbsp;&nbsp; said that because of their constant complaining&nbsp; and unreasonable demands, they directly contributed&nbsp;&nbsp; to J-Hope's worries and concerns, which ended&nbsp; up being confirmed in the Suchwita episode and in&nbsp;&nbsp; the 'thanks to' message that I mentioned earlier in&nbsp; the video. And in typical solo stan fashion, they tried&nbsp;&nbsp; to claim that we were spreading misinformation.&nbsp; Misinformation. Even though J-Hope was the one&nbsp;&nbsp; who said these words. Their reasoning for why&nbsp; it was misinformation was that the Weverse&nbsp;&nbsp; translation translated what he said differently,&nbsp; so let's read the Weverse translation, shall we?&nbsp;&nbsp; the Weverse translation of that same excerpt says, "I had so many worries when the album first released.&nbsp;&nbsp; Why aren't there that many promotions? Why aren't&nbsp; you dancing? But it's understandable to hear this&nbsp;&nbsp; kind of feedback. I think I had a lot of worries&nbsp; in regards to that, but after some time passed, the&nbsp;&nbsp; fans liked it as well. I seem to be ending it well."&nbsp; As you can see, both of these translations pretty&nbsp;&nbsp; much say the same thing. But solo stans tried to&nbsp; pass off the Weverse translation as J-Hope's own&nbsp;&nbsp; worries, simply because the Weverse translation&nbsp; didn't include quotation marks around the&nbsp;&nbsp; questions. However, they ignored the other half of&nbsp; his sentence where he said, "But it's understandable&nbsp;&nbsp; to hear this kind of feedback", indicating that&nbsp; the aformentioned questions were feedback he&nbsp;&nbsp; received and not J-Hope's own personal concerns.&nbsp; No matter which translation you use, both of them&nbsp;&nbsp; say that when Jack In The Box was first released, J-Hope got feedback concerning the lack of promotion&nbsp;&nbsp; as well as a lack of choreography that he was&nbsp; typically known for. Due to this feedback, he had&nbsp;&nbsp; worries that this album wouldn't be well-received,&nbsp; but the fans liked it, so he considered Jack In The Box&nbsp;&nbsp; a success. However, solo stans called what&nbsp; he said "misinformation" and harassed ARMYs into&nbsp;&nbsp; deleting their posts about it, spreading their own&nbsp; version of what they thought he was talking about.&nbsp;&nbsp; Luckily, many ARMYs weren't budging and refused to&nbsp;let them take control of the narrative so easily.&nbsp;&nbsp; Solo stans tried to silence them by saying that they&nbsp; were just trying to enjoy J-Hope's birthday in peace&nbsp;&nbsp; (yes, all of this went down on J-Hope's birthday) but&nbsp;like these ARMYs have said, solo stans were the&nbsp;&nbsp; ones broadcasting their complaints for everyone to&nbsp; see since before Jack In The Box was even released.&nbsp; It's only now that ARMYs want to hold solo stans&nbsp; accountable for the stress they gave J-Hope that&nbsp;&nbsp; they start vying for sympathy and claiming how&nbsp; they just--quote and unquote--"want things to be over".&nbsp;&nbsp; Since July of 2022, J-Hope solo stans have done&nbsp; nothing but be loud about their problems and&nbsp;&nbsp; drag these issues out for months on end. There has&nbsp;never been a point in time when they wanted to&nbsp;&nbsp; end things quickly and quietly. Another thing&nbsp; solo stans tried to backtrack on is claiming&nbsp;&nbsp; that they never said anything about having an&nbsp; issue with Jack In The Box promotions and that&nbsp;&nbsp; their only issues were with the formatting of the&nbsp; physical album, but if you go back to older tweets,&nbsp;&nbsp; you'll see that this is also untrue. So many of&nbsp; them did talk about how they were disappointed&nbsp;&nbsp; in the fact that there was supposedly no&nbsp; promotional activities and no choreography&nbsp;&nbsp; for the title track, but they just don't want to&nbsp; accept that their complaints made J-Hope worry.&nbsp;&nbsp; They don't want to accept that they can have a&nbsp; negative impact on their favorite member.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is why, despite the fact that J-Hope's solo stans have been consistently caught out in their&nbsp;lies,&nbsp; they won't ever truly apologize, nor would I&nbsp; be surprised if they didn't. This is simply just&nbsp;&nbsp; how solo stans operate. Instead of admitting to&nbsp; ARMYs that they were wrong, they'd sooner just&nbsp;&nbsp; find any way to deflect blame and keep doing&nbsp; what they're doing. They'll constantly go out&nbsp;&nbsp; of their way to not listen to BTS when they show&nbsp; appreciation to their fans, their staff, and each&nbsp;other,&nbsp; calling BTS liars and confused whenever it&nbsp; suits them, but in the next breath will swear up&nbsp;&nbsp; and down that nobody supports their favorite&nbsp; member, as if these aren't the same people who&nbsp;&nbsp; would turn on that member if he said anything that&nbsp; proves them wrong. The lack of self-awareness/ willful ignorance solo stans have is absolutely&nbsp; astounding. So if solo stans don't listen to Big Hit,&nbsp;&nbsp; they don't listen to ARMYs, and they don't&nbsp; listen to BTS themselves, then what's their endgame? We've discussed what solo&nbsp;stans are and the type of things they do, but&nbsp;&nbsp; we haven't discussed why solo stans do these&nbsp; things. Why latch onto a specific member in&nbsp;&nbsp; a group and believe they've been mistreated for&nbsp; their whole career? Why believe that their company&nbsp;&nbsp; has been out to get them since day one? Why go&nbsp; against ARMYs and even your favorite member to&nbsp;&nbsp; crusade in the name of justice? Before I start, I&nbsp; want to give another disclaimer that everything&nbsp;&nbsp; I list from here on out is simply speculation&nbsp; based on observation. BTS solo stans are tens&nbsp;&nbsp; of thousands maybe even hundreds of thousands&nbsp; in number, so there won't be a one-size-fits-all&nbsp;&nbsp; explanation for why all of them are solo stans.&nbsp; With that being said, let's get to my tinfoil hat&nbsp;&nbsp; theories. The first reason why I think people are&nbsp; solo stans could be because they were previously&nbsp;&nbsp; or still are a fan of other groups, and due to&nbsp; the way that some K-pop companies mismanage&nbsp;&nbsp; their idols, perhaps that malice towards those&nbsp; specific companies transferred over. That could&nbsp;&nbsp; explain why solo stans hate Big Hit so much&nbsp; and don't trust their words, even though they've&nbsp;&nbsp; shown proof that--while not perfect--they genuinely&nbsp; care about the well-being and success of their&nbsp;&nbsp; artists. Or, it could be that because they're&nbsp; from other fandoms, maybe they only like one&nbsp;&nbsp; particular member of BTS but would rather die&nbsp; than become an ARMY or follow BTS as a group&nbsp;&nbsp; on social media, so as a compromise, they just join&nbsp; the solo stan community, where they know they'll&nbsp;&nbsp; only be getting updates on the member they like&nbsp; and they can be free to hate on the other members&nbsp;&nbsp; and attack ARMYs unprovoked. A second reason I&nbsp; can think of is that some solo stans are just&nbsp;&nbsp; naive people who got tricked into believing that a&nbsp; specific member is being mistreated. Despite their&nbsp;&nbsp; narratives being easy to debunk, there are so many&nbsp; solo stans for different members of BTS. Why? Well... My best bet is that it's because a lot of people&nbsp; who got to BTS are new to K-pop in general, and so&nbsp;&nbsp; they're unfamiliar with the concept of solo stans.&nbsp; Therefore, they can't differentiate between solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stan narratives and the actual truth. That's&nbsp; how solo stans dupe newer fans into believing&nbsp;&nbsp; the lies they tell and suck them into the solo&nbsp; stan community. The third reason I have for you&nbsp;&nbsp; is that maybe solo stans do this to feel like&nbsp; a hero. Liking a specific member's music and/or&nbsp;&nbsp; personality, I feel, is only part of the solo stan&nbsp; experience. The other part is campaigning for&nbsp;&nbsp; your favorite member to be treated better. Sending&nbsp; email templates, making threads that victimize&nbsp;&nbsp; members of BTS--these are all ways in which solo&nbsp; stans feed their white knight complex, They want&nbsp;&nbsp; to save somebody and feel special for doing&nbsp; it, They want to be known as the ones who knew&nbsp;&nbsp; what was going on all along and fought for their&nbsp; favorite member to be noticed. A lot of people, in&nbsp;&nbsp; response to seeing the shenanigans of solo stans,&nbsp; tend to respond with: "Well, if you're only gonna&nbsp;&nbsp; stan one member, why not just become a fan of&nbsp; actual soloists in the K-music industry?" But, in&nbsp;&nbsp; my opinion, it was never just about the music for&nbsp; solo stans, it's also about the validation they&nbsp;&nbsp; get from stanning someone who they've deemed to&nbsp; be underappreciated in a group. Korean soloists&nbsp;&nbsp; don't have to fight for their place because, well,&nbsp; they're soloists. They don't have to share lines&nbsp;&nbsp; or screentime with anybody else. So, by stanning&nbsp; soloists, solo stans would have nothing to crusade&nbsp;for.&nbsp; Alternatively, a fourth reason solo stans&nbsp; could do what they do could possibly be for&nbsp;&nbsp; status. I've seen solo stans pick who they believe&nbsp; is the most popular member and lord it over ARMYs' heads.&nbsp;&nbsp; And if we disagree with the way they operate,&nbsp; then it's because apparently we're jealous of how&nbsp;&nbsp; popular their favorite member is to the point&nbsp; where we don't want to see them succeed.&nbsp;&nbsp; The fifth reason why I think solo stans do what they&nbsp;do is actually from a theory an ARMY came up with online. Basically, the theory is that solo stans&nbsp; live vicariously through their favorite member&nbsp;&nbsp; because they latch onto people with personality&nbsp; traits they're lacking in. This could perhaps&nbsp;&nbsp; explain why solo stans tend to be the opposite&nbsp; of the person they're a fan of. Where a member is&nbsp;&nbsp; kind, their solo stans are rude. Where a member&nbsp; is friendly, their solo stans are antisocial.&nbsp;&nbsp; Where a member forgives, their solo stans hold&nbsp; pointless grudges. You probably get my point by now.&nbsp;&nbsp; The final (and probably the saddest) reason why&nbsp; I think solo stans could do what they do is that&nbsp;&nbsp; some solo stans maybe just have unchecked&nbsp; issues. Maybe there are solo stans who lead&nbsp;&nbsp; lives offline that are not so great, and they're&nbsp; not really in control of their circumstances. So&nbsp;&nbsp; they project their anger and insecurities onto&nbsp; their favorite idol in order to feel in control&nbsp;&nbsp; of somebody's life, even if that life is not their&nbsp; own. I remember coming across a thread once, talking&nbsp;&nbsp; about how the language that solo stans use is&nbsp; akin to language used in abusive relationships.&nbsp;&nbsp; And, when I sat down to think about it, they were&nbsp; right. I looked up signs of abuse online just to&nbsp;&nbsp; see how much of this statement was true, and&nbsp; the behavior of solo stans does indeed match up&nbsp;&nbsp; with a lot of characteristics of emotional abuse.&nbsp; Solo stans constantly attempt to put it in their&nbsp;&nbsp; favorite members' heads that they're the only ones&nbsp; who love them and appreciate them, and that no one&nbsp;&nbsp; but solo stans understand their true value. They&nbsp; use this narrative to justify speaking for/over&nbsp;&nbsp; their favorite member because they assert that&nbsp; they're the only ones who know what's best for&nbsp;&nbsp; them, despite never meeting Big Hit staff, never&nbsp; reading BTS's contracts, and never meeting BTS&nbsp;&nbsp; themselves. Another thing to keep in mind is that&nbsp; abusive behavior is learned behavior. So chances are&nbsp;&nbsp; these solo stans that display abusive tendencies&nbsp; have most likely had an upbringing where they've&nbsp;&nbsp; either witnessed or been a victim of abuse and&nbsp; are now displaying that type of behavior with&nbsp;&nbsp; their favorite member... whether they're aware of&nbsp; it or not. Then solo stans find communities on&nbsp;&nbsp; social platforms where they congregate in swarms&nbsp; and validate each other's problematic tendencies.&nbsp;&nbsp; Twitter is the ideal place for solo stans to&nbsp; set up camp, as the very formatting of it is&nbsp;&nbsp; like playing an arduous game of telephone. This&nbsp; is how narratives are allowed to spread online&nbsp;&nbsp; without knowing where certain information came&nbsp; from, or if said information is even correct.&nbsp;&nbsp; And the growth of these communities is what leads&nbsp;to problems that have real-life consequences&nbsp;&nbsp; on both the members and the outside perception&nbsp; of the fandom, such as the truck incidents or&nbsp;&nbsp; the harassment of innocent ARMYs online.&nbsp; Like the thread says, it's sabotaging the&nbsp;&nbsp; members' professional relationships with other&nbsp; people, and that type of thing can damage BTS's career. So far, I hope this video has opened your&nbsp; eyes as to how dangerous solo stans can be, but&nbsp;&nbsp; I thought it would be useful if I gave you some&nbsp; tips on how to identify them. When it comes to&nbsp;&nbsp; identifying solo stans, there are four things I&nbsp; would suggest you look out for. The first thing&nbsp;&nbsp; I would suggest you look out for is their acccount&nbsp; layout. The thing about solo stans is that they&nbsp;&nbsp; are usually pretty overt with how they identify.&nbsp; They don't try to hide the fact that they are&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stans. So for most situations, their social&nbsp; media layout and bio will give it away.&nbsp;&nbsp; Aside from obviously having a profile picture and&nbsp; username directly related to their favorite&nbsp;&nbsp; member, their bio will often tell you what member&nbsp; the account is dedicated to. It's not uncommon for&nbsp;&nbsp; them to directly state in their bios that they're&nbsp; solo stans either, so that's something else you&nbsp;&nbsp; should keep in mind. However, these days, there&nbsp; have been more and more solo stans attempting&nbsp;&nbsp; to cosplay as ARMYs to gain sizable platforms&nbsp; before showing their true colors, so I would also&nbsp;&nbsp; suggest that you keep that in mind as well and&nbsp; continue to be critical of the accounts you see&nbsp;&nbsp; on your timelines. The second thing to look out&nbsp; for is their following list. On social media, not&nbsp;&nbsp; only can you see who follows certain accounts, but&nbsp; you can also see who those accounts are following.&nbsp;&nbsp; So if you suspected somebody of being a solo stan and you still can't be 100% sure just from looking&nbsp;&nbsp; at their page layout, then you should also check&nbsp; who they're following. Solo stans only care about&nbsp;&nbsp; the member that they claim to stan, so all the&nbsp; accounts they follow will be related to their&nbsp;&nbsp; favorite member, too. An example of accounts you&nbsp; might see in their following list are member-focused&nbsp;&nbsp; news and chart accounts, member-focused translation&nbsp;accounts, and even member-focused picture accounts.&nbsp;&nbsp; You will never see them following accounts that&nbsp; are dedicated to or are inclusive of other members&nbsp;&nbsp; of BTS. It would also do you good to check these&nbsp; accounts' likes and retweets. A lot of solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; swear they don't disrespect the other members but&nbsp; will constantly like and share posts by accounts&nbsp;&nbsp; that are openly shady towards the other members&nbsp; of BTS. The third thing to look out for is their&nbsp;&nbsp; attitude towards BTS, Big Hit and HYBE, and ARMYs. Does the account in question constantly push a&nbsp;&nbsp; narrative that a particular member is unhappy&nbsp; within the group or that the other members dislike&nbsp;&nbsp; him and/or are jealous of him? Do they believe&nbsp; the other members are holding him back and that&nbsp;&nbsp; he'd be better off going solo? Do they believe&nbsp; that a particular member is consistently being&nbsp;&nbsp; mistreated by Big Hit in comparison to the other&nbsp; members, and therefore resent the label as a result?&nbsp;&nbsp; Do they claim that ARMYs show a bias against&nbsp; a particular member?
