If the answer to these&nbsp;&nbsp; questions is yes, then you are dealing with a solo&nbsp; stan. And if they're not a solo stan yet, they're&nbsp;&nbsp; definitely on their way to becoming one. The last&nbsp; thing to look out for is overall demeanor and tweet&nbsp;patterns.&nbsp; A key thing to note about solo stans is&nbsp; that they're never happy. All they do is constantly&nbsp;&nbsp; spread negativity and make social media a living&nbsp; hell, whether it's victimizing and infantilizing&nbsp;&nbsp; their favorite member, attacking ARMYs over&nbsp; non-issues, or sending hate towards BTS's staff&nbsp;&nbsp; and anyone who's worked with them, like other music&nbsp;artists and outside producers. For solo stans it's&nbsp;&nbsp; like there's always something wrong. Something else&nbsp;they like doing is trending hashtags and keywords&nbsp;&nbsp; when they want to spread awareness on something&nbsp;that they've deemed an issue. If you've ever been&nbsp;&nbsp; on the Twitter trending page and saw something&nbsp; trending like "Respect [insert BTS member here]" or&nbsp;&nbsp; "Free [insert BTS member here]", they are pretty much&nbsp;always started by solo stans. And something also&nbsp;&nbsp; commonly done by solo stans is extreme public&nbsp; demonstrations, such as sending a truck to the&nbsp;&nbsp; HYBE company building. Solo stans love to do these&nbsp;things because it gets them attention from media&nbsp;&nbsp; outlets, who will then report about it, which will&nbsp; give BTS's label bad publicity, which is what solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stans want, at the end of the day. Also, if on top of&nbsp; this, they support sasaengs and fansites, that's another&nbsp;&nbsp; red flag for reasons I've already mentioned. I also&nbsp; have to mention that in isolation, supporting sasaengs&nbsp;&nbsp; and fansites does not mean that someone is&nbsp; a solo stan but there are a lot of solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; who do support them, so it's just another thing to&nbsp; look out for. Although even if they weren't a solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stan, anyone supporting such things is bad, in&nbsp; my books. And another thing. In terms of demeanor&nbsp;&nbsp; and tweet patterns, something you will often see is&nbsp; that solo stans tend to have a very performative&nbsp;&nbsp; way of measuring how much you stan somebody that&nbsp;revolves around your activity on social media.&nbsp;&nbsp; What I mean by this is that they'll do stuff like look&nbsp; up your account name in the Twitter search engine&nbsp;&nbsp; along with specific keywords that relate to their&nbsp; favorite member, and if no results come up, they'll&nbsp;&nbsp; use it to say "See? How can you say you support&nbsp; my favorite member if you don't tweet about&nbsp;him?"&nbsp; I hate this method for three reasons. The&nbsp; first reason is that this method isn't even a&nbsp;&nbsp; reliable way to check if somebody's tweeted about&nbsp; something. There are plenty of ways to tweet about&nbsp;&nbsp; a BTS member without actually typing their names&nbsp; out or other things like names of specific songs.&nbsp;&nbsp; For exampl,e I could tweet the sentence "I love&nbsp; Yoongi." and that would show up in a Twitter search&nbsp;engine if I looked up my tweets using "Yoongi" as a&nbsp; keyword. But if I tweet a picture of Yoongi with the&nbsp;&nbsp; caption "I love him." that's not going to show up&nbsp; in the search results if you search up my tweets&nbsp;&nbsp; using that same keyword. Not to mention that this&nbsp; method doesn't even take likes and retweets into&nbsp;&nbsp; consideration that will obviously not show up if&nbsp; someone looks up your tweets either because the&nbsp;&nbsp; tweets that you're liking and sharing to your page&nbsp; are from other accounts. The second reason why I&nbsp;&nbsp; hate this method is is that it also doesn't take&nbsp; into consideration people who are less active on&nbsp;&nbsp; social media. Just because someone has an account on Twitter it doesn't mean that they're using it 24/7.&nbsp;&nbsp; Some people only come on Twitter every once&nbsp; in a while and sometimes even less so, but because&nbsp;&nbsp; they don't have enough tweets about a certain&nbsp; member for your liking, it means that they're&nbsp;&nbsp; an anti? There are so many people busy with&nbsp; things like school or work or kids, or maybe they&nbsp;&nbsp; just have other hobbies outside of Twitter. Most&nbsp; people don't have time to be active on Twitter&nbsp;&nbsp; using specific keywords and trending specific&nbsp; hashtags every second of the day, and those who&nbsp;&nbsp; expect them to have such a chronically online&nbsp; mindset. The third reason why I hate this method&nbsp;&nbsp; is simply because it's used to police people&nbsp; in the fandom and tell them how to tweet on&nbsp;&nbsp; their own page. At the end of the day, not every&nbsp; account on ARMY Twitter is a professional fanbase.&nbsp;&nbsp; Some people are here to connect with other&nbsp; fans, some people are here to share their artwork,&nbsp;&nbsp; and some people only made a Twitter account to&nbsp; keep up with BTS related news. ARMYs should not&nbsp;&nbsp; feel obligated to tweet in specific ways to prove&nbsp; that they support the members. You're not going to&nbsp;&nbsp; find me going out of my way to tweet hashtags&nbsp; just for the sake of it, but you will find BTS&nbsp;&nbsp; at the top of my Spotify Wrapped leaderboard. At&nbsp; the end of the day, it's not the hashtags or&nbsp;&nbsp; the keywords you trend that will make a tangible&nbsp; impact, it's supporting the music. So when people&nbsp;&nbsp; unironically use this tweet-searching method&nbsp; to deduce whether someone supports a particular&nbsp;&nbsp; member of BTS or not, it just frustrates me&nbsp; so much because it literally doesn't even work. I've talked about my issues with solo stans,&nbsp; why they do what they do, and how to identify them,&nbsp;&nbsp; but I wouldn't consider this video complete if&nbsp; I didn't give you some advice; not just about&nbsp;&nbsp; how to avoid becoming a solo stan, but how to&nbsp; avoid enabling them and giving them a platform.&nbsp;&nbsp; Tip number one: avoid following member-related&nbsp; topics. This is more of a Twitter-specific piece&nbsp;&nbsp; of advice, but it's still important for me to say&nbsp; this because Twitter is the social media platform&nbsp;&nbsp; that probably has the most concentrated number&nbsp; of ARMYs on it. On Twitter, you are able to follow&nbsp;&nbsp; certain topics which allows you to see tweets&nbsp; related to that topic on your timeline. The way&nbsp;&nbsp; this works is through the algorithm categorizing&nbsp; tweets based on specific keywords that may pertain&nbsp;&nbsp; to the topic in question. And while this may be&nbsp; a useful tool to get quick news on things like&nbsp;&nbsp; gaming or fashion, solo stans tend to utilize&nbsp; the topic feature by using specific keywords to&nbsp;&nbsp; trend solo stan rhetoric under topics related&nbsp; to their favorite member. So if any of you are&nbsp;&nbsp; following certain member topics like 'Suga' or&nbsp; 'RM', you should go and unfollow those topics.&nbsp;&nbsp; I'd even recommend unfollowing the general BTS topic, just to be extra safe. Instead of relying on the&nbsp;&nbsp; algorithm to recommend you tweets under a topic, it would be better for you to carefully curate your&nbsp;&nbsp; space by individually selecting each ARMY account&nbsp; you follow on here so that you know that the only&nbsp;&nbsp; tweets showing up on your timeline are from people&nbsp;that you know for a fact aren't solo stans, diet&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stans, or any other type of undesirable.&nbsp; Tip number two, take everything on your feed&nbsp;&nbsp; with a grain of salt. Form a habit of fact-checking&nbsp; when it comes to information about BTS, Big Hit, or&nbsp;ARMYs. If someone tells you something, your first&nbsp; instinct should be to ask for receipts. Never take&nbsp;&nbsp; someone's word as fact unless you know 100% that&nbsp;it happened. Another thing you should do if you end&nbsp;&nbsp; up getting receipts is making sure that they're&nbsp; receipts with full context. Something that solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stans always do is take tweets out of context&nbsp; and twist them to suit their narrative. So always&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure that if someone sends you a screenshot&nbsp; of something in the attempt to paint a person or&nbsp;&nbsp; an event in a certain way, always push to get the&nbsp; full context of the situation. And if they won't&nbsp;&nbsp; give it to you, look for it yourself or ask around.&nbsp; I've said this before on Twitter, but solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; thrive off people who don't do research, so by&nbsp; double-checking your sources, you can reduce their&nbsp;influence.&nbsp; Tip number three, watch BTS's content&nbsp; on your own. The content posted by BTS and Big Hit&nbsp;&nbsp; are the best sources to draw your knowledge from&nbsp; when it comes to the boys and their relationship&nbsp;&nbsp; with their fans, the company, and each other. Nothing&nbsp;can tell you that story better than BTS themselves.&nbsp;&nbsp; We as ARMYs are fortunate enough that BTS have&nbsp; documented so much of their lives on camera, and&nbsp;&nbsp; that their company is relatively transparent with&nbsp; how they operate. So I urge everyone, especially&nbsp;&nbsp; newer ARMYs to dive into their content and&nbsp; see for yourself what they're about. Don't let&nbsp;&nbsp; people's opinions on the timeline influence how&nbsp; you see them. Tip number four, if solo stans try to&nbsp;&nbsp; say that you offended them in some way, do not&nbsp; cater to their feelings. As long as you haven't&nbsp;&nbsp; said anything wrong, don't offer clarifications on&nbsp; your tweets for them, don't apologize to them, and&nbsp;&nbsp; certainly don't delete your tweets either. If you&nbsp; do, then it gives solo stans the message that if&nbsp;&nbsp; enough of them bombard you with hate, they can&nbsp; make you bend to their whim. What I suggest you&nbsp;&nbsp; do if you're getting unreasonably attacked&nbsp; by solo stans is mute the tweet (or tweets)&nbsp;&nbsp; in question and close your DMs. When they don't&nbsp; have a way to directly contact you, they can't&nbsp;&nbsp; get into your head. Also, I would recommend setting&nbsp;up two-step verification on Twitter because I've&nbsp;&nbsp; had experiences where solo stans have tried to&nbsp; get into my account after I made a tweet they&nbsp;&nbsp; didn't like, so the link on how to set this up will&nbsp; be in the description below. Tip number five, if you&nbsp;&nbsp; see solo stan rhetoric on the timeline, call it out.&nbsp; Don't just block them and ignore it. Solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; have become such a big issue in the past couple of&nbsp; years because we ignored them and hoped that they&nbsp;&nbsp; would fade back into the shadows. What happened&nbsp; instead is they grew without needing to worry&nbsp;&nbsp; about us refuting their narratives. On top of that,&nbsp; by publicly calling them out, newer ARMYs have the&nbsp;&nbsp; opportunity to see for themselves how we deal with&nbsp;solo stans and can learn from us. Ignoring them is&nbsp;&nbsp; just not enough. We may have tricked ourselves&nbsp; into thinking it worked in the past, but in the&nbsp;&nbsp; long run, it's not sustainable. As far as I'm&nbsp; concerned, solo stans are nothing more than&nbsp;&nbsp; weeds and this garden that we call our fandom.&nbsp; And if your garden has weeds, are you going to&nbsp;&nbsp; ignore them and hope that they'll go away, or are&nbsp; you going to go into your shed and bust out the&nbsp;herbicide?&nbsp; You tell me. Tip number six, don't make&nbsp; excuses for solo stans... just don't. Saying that&nbsp;&nbsp; they can stay "as long as they don't disrespect&nbsp; the other members" is a stupid argument because&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stans' very existence is disrespecting&nbsp; BTS's wishes for people to love and support&nbsp;&nbsp; all seven of them. The second you express sympathy&nbsp;for solo stans, they'll latch on to you and try&nbsp;&nbsp; to control you. Solo stans have no affiliation with&nbsp; BTS whatsoever. These are not people you want to&nbsp;&nbsp; side with and you are not obligated to cater to&nbsp; their feelings. Catering to their feelings is what&nbsp;&nbsp; got us here in the first place. If anything, we&nbsp; should be pushing for more people to deny solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stans, especially member-focused accounts. A lot of&nbsp;ARMYs are unknowingly following accounts that may&nbsp;&nbsp; seem innocent because they're doing something like&nbsp;posting cute pictures of a particular BTS member,&nbsp;&nbsp; but these accounts allow solo stans to follow&nbsp; them and influence their behavior and beliefs.&nbsp;&nbsp; We need to start working to hold accounts of&nbsp; all sizes, well, accountable. And if they can't&nbsp;&nbsp; take responsibility, then we unfollow them and go&nbsp; follow accounts that are more than welcome to call&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stans and diet solo stans out. Last but&nbsp; not least, tip number seven. Just trust the boys.&nbsp; BTS are a group of seven grown men who are currently&nbsp;in their mid 20s to early 30s, and they're only&nbsp;&nbsp; going to get older from here on out. They are not&nbsp; children, and they are fully capable of expressing&nbsp;&nbsp; discomfort if they do not enjoy something or&nbsp; are feeling wronged by their label. Don't you&nbsp;&nbsp; think for a second that they need us fans to&nbsp; overstep and go on a witch hunt for anyone on&nbsp;&nbsp; their behalf. They know what's best for them, and if&nbsp; they re-signed to their company for an extra seven years,&nbsp;&nbsp; it means that they have faith that Big Hit will treat&nbsp; them with the respect they deserve. If worse comes&nbsp;&nbsp; to worst, then BTS have their own legal consultants&nbsp; outside the company. Basically, what I'm trying to&nbsp;&nbsp; say is they don't need us to send trucks, write&nbsp; emails, and trend hashtags for every non-issue&nbsp;&nbsp; under the sun. BTS can decide for themselves if and&nbsp;when they want to discuss an issue. It's not our&nbsp;&nbsp; place as fans to decide that for them. If you're&nbsp; an ARMY and you trust BTS, then to an extent, you&nbsp;&nbsp; also need to trust their label. Trust does not&nbsp; mean blind devotion. If Big Hit or HYBE messes up,&nbsp;&nbsp; then we as fans have a right to call that out.&nbsp; But constantly eyeing these companies under a&nbsp;&nbsp; lens of unwarranted suspicion is not helpful and&nbsp; only increases paranoia. Have faith that BTS made&nbsp;&nbsp; the right choice. I promise you, you will feel so&nbsp; much more at peace if you put your faith in BTS's decisions. To conclude this video, solo stans--whether we like it or not--cause so many problems&nbsp;&nbsp; in the fandom that I feel a lot of people don't&nbsp; even realize. Back in the days when our fandom&nbsp;&nbsp; was smaller, it was easier to pretend that they&nbsp; weren't there, but now, solo stans can do real damage&nbsp;&nbsp; past trending hashtags. They're organized to the&nbsp; point where they can use their presence to pull&nbsp;&nbsp; money together for questionable projects and bully&nbsp; anyone who disagrees with their methods or their&nbsp;&nbsp; opinions, even if it means bullying the members&nbsp; of BTS themselves. I feel like a big reason why&nbsp;&nbsp; solo stans have not been backed into a corner yet&nbsp; is because of the fact that they're not given the&nbsp;&nbsp; same treatment as akgaes. BTS have said out of their own mouths that they want us to love and appreciate&nbsp;&nbsp; all seven members, yet people still justify&nbsp; the existence of solo stans because they're&nbsp;&nbsp; not akgaes. And this lack of urgency to purge the&nbsp; fandom of solo stans is what gives solo stans&nbsp;&nbsp; opportunities to gain foothold into important&nbsp; conversations regarding the boys. The biggest issue&nbsp;&nbsp; is that the solo stan community is seen as this&nbsp; separate entity that exists outside the boundaries&nbsp;&nbsp; of our fandom, but it really isn't. People keep&nbsp; worrying that one day solo stans will infiltrate&nbsp;&nbsp; our fandom, but the harsh reality is that they're&nbsp; already here. Solo stans and diet solos with&nbsp;&nbsp; thousands of followers don't just come from thin&nbsp; air. Real people in the fandom are giving solo&nbsp;&nbsp; stans a platform out of naivety or out of fear of&nbsp; being targeted by them and labeled as an OT6. As a&nbsp;&nbsp; result, solo stans have managed to worm their way&nbsp; into our fandom and build up their platforms from&nbsp;&nbsp; the inside to spread their harmful narratives. And&nbsp; if this keeps up, the fandom division will only&nbsp;&nbsp; get worse. Going forward, if we want the solo stan&nbsp; problem to disappear, we need to start looking&nbsp;&nbsp; at solo stans for what they are: antis. They are&nbsp; anti-ARMY, anti-Big Hit, and, most importantly, anti-BTS.&nbsp;&nbsp; Every time you like a post from a solo stan,&nbsp; you are liking the post of an anti. Every time&nbsp;&nbsp; you agree with a solo stan, you are agreeing&nbsp; with an anti. Every time you defend the opinion&nbsp;&nbsp; of a solo stan, you are defending the opinion of&nbsp; an anti. I really want to drive this point home&nbsp;&nbsp; because I want people to realize who exactly&nbsp; they're giving a platform to when they engage&nbsp;&nbsp; with these solo stans. You're giving a platform&nbsp; to the very same people who don't even listen&nbsp;&nbsp; to the words of the people they claim to stan.&nbsp; Their opinions on anything regarding the career&nbsp;&nbsp; of any of the members are invalid and should be&nbsp; treated as such. All in all, we as a fandom need&nbsp;&nbsp; to be better at standing our ground against them.&nbsp; Solo stans may be big, but we are bigger. drug marketing and so innovations to drugs occurred in the 19th century there were isolation of specific alkaloids that enhanced the potency of different substances so in 1804 morphine is isolated from opium in 1832 codeine is isolated from opium and in 1855 cocaine is isolated from the cocoa plant and what this does is creates more powerful drugs the second innovation was in drug administration in 1853 the hypodermic needle was invented and there's always unintended consequences when there's innovation and so one of the unintended consequences was overdose and ironically sadly however you want to look at it the first known morphine overdose was the guy's wife you know can you imagine inventing something that ends up killing your your spouse um the third innovation was in drug marketing so in the late 1800s there was a real growth in newspaper advertising that fueled what was called the patent medicine industry and there was also the advent of mail order catalogs which were selling drugs and drug injection equipment directly to people in their homes so you would see advertisements such as this this is mrs winslow's soothing syrup and it's targeted towards children it's for children's teething what it doesn't tell you is what's in it what the main ingredient was was morphine uh this is stickney and pores uh pure perigoric and you can see it's uh has a certain amount of alcohol in it a certain amount of opium in it per each fluid ounce and on the back side it tells you how to use it five days old five drops two weeks old eight drops five years old 25 drops adults a teaspoon full addicts a bottle or two [Laughter] this is an advertisement from 1885 advertising cocaine and it's for sale by all druggists which was actually the drugstore the pharmacy you could go to the pharmacy and purchase cocaine 1898 there's this kind of rise in addiction and this problem is becoming apparent so in 1898 bear releases a safe non-addictive alternative to morphine it goes by the name of heroin so when when addicts or persons with addiction talk about kind of having a history of distrust for pharmaceutical companies and so forth it has a long well-founded uh history in that distrust during this same era you would start to see addiction cures and one of the first addiction cures is something that many people with addiction have tried which is to treat one problem with using something else so this doctor in kentucky in 1878 began to treat morphine addiction with cocaine okay and so whenever you're doing something you want to know how do i know this is working right so so this doctor prescribed 25 pounds of cocaine in a two-year period and he was convinced of his success that he discovered something he'd prescribed a pound of cocaine to a woman and he wrote i received a note from her when she had used this she was much encouraged and had ordered two pounds more it says i saw her recently when she assured me she had no desire for morphine we're still trying to track this down we've heard unverified reports she lived in ann arbor and used to walk to kentucky to get this filled but we're still trying to but the the sad the tragedy in this is that the same patent medicine industry that was contributing to the rise in addiction began to generate miracle cures and so people would be at a point where they realize that they have a problem and they want something different in their life and they would see advertisements like this morphine easy home cure painless and permanent or you'd see one like this from sears and roebuck and it says it contains no poisonous narcotics and it says immediately on taking a dose of this remedy a calming and soothing effect is produced it sounds a little bit like an opiate it acts as a tonic to the nerves its use will completely destroy that terrible craving for morphine and so what was discovered is that all these miracle cures that were being sold sold to people who were addicted all contain morphine sometimes at higher levels than what they were already taking so you had people who were in a place of desperation were seeking help and were really just kind of ending uh continuing to be addicted so in 1905 there was an expose on patent medicines and it appeared in collier's magazine and it kind of resulted in the pure food and drug act in 1906 and you got to label what's in in your prescriptions and preparations and so forth so that's a little bit of the kind of the factors that contributed to the rise of addiction and now the next thing is kind of what really contributed to the criminalization of addiction and if you want to look at how persons with alcoholism and persons with addiction kind of veered in very different directions so in 1914 there was a act passed called the harrison anti-narcotic act and and it was used as a registration and taxation bill to restrict the use of opiates and cocaine to legitimate medical purposes so in essence a law was passed that said you could no longer go to the store or to the pharmacy and purchase narcotics or cocaine you couldn't order them through catalogs you could only get them from physicians at this point and i think if we looked at that in today's time it would be probably similar if congress passed a law saying you could only nicotine products through prescription from a physician right that would it would completely change the way the entire population has access to substances that they've become dependent upon so america went from kind of uncontrolled access to access regulated by physicians the treasury department enforced this bill and they interpreted it in a way that was not the intention of how the bill was written so it was originally designed as a revenue bill the treasury department starts dictating to physicians how they can prescribe and they tell physicians that they can no longer maintain an addict on opiates they have to taper them off uh some physicians continue to prescribe in a way that they thought was consistent with their best medical judgment but in 1919 a supreme court ruling ruled in favor of kind of the treasury department and as a result it really became against the law for physicians to maintain anyone on morphine or another opiate and what happened as a result of this is that physicians began to get prosecuted and in big numbers so more than 25 000 physicians were indicted under the harrison act 3 000 physicians went to jail for their prescribing practices 20 000 paid substantial fines and physicians at that time stopped treating addicted patients and and i will point out that there are local communities that were kind of trying to figure out what do we do now and so some local communities like in new york city set up morphine clinics and this is decades before methadone maintenance clinics but they set up morphine clinics where persons who are addicted could go and get a daily dose of morphine so this is the worst street morphing clinic in new york city and this is one in new orleans with a female person who's addicted getting her daily morphine dose there was a vacuum for anyone who is addicted between 1924 and 1935 the federal government had come in said you can't have morphine clinics and those closed down there was a collapse of private treatment facilities there was increased prosecution and incarceration of addicts and so the question becomes what is what did treatment look like so if you were addicted during this time period what would that look like for you and so there were invasive cures the serum therapies where they would raise blisters on an addict's abdomen and thighs withdraw fluid from the blisters and re-inject into the person during withdrawal there were bromide sleep treatments where one in five who took it ended up dying from the treatment there were aversion therapies if you think about ant abuses and inversion therapy they used one with succinylcholine where if you took that it you'd get the sensation that you were suffocating and they would pair that with an opiate to try to make you learn that if i take this and i can't breathe there was more kind of invasive trends in in many places but in north carolina where boyd and i come from there there was sterilization for what was termed the mentally unfit and this disproportionately impacted women particularly women who were institutionalized and could only guarantee their voluntary freedom and release if they agreed to have a hysterectomy or be sterilized uh there were insulin therapies at that time for morphine
